Although, in your case, the error seems to stem from the compilation of the _graphcut_ extension. You can try to install medpy without it. Simply download the source code and replace in _setup.py_ the 14 lines below

`### INSTALLATION`

with 

`run_setup(False)`

and run the setup with

> python setup.py install

which should trigger the medpy installation omitting the graphcut compilation step.

I hope that solves your problems.

Thanks for reporting this.

It appears to be an issue coming in via `urllib3` and what it's doing in the face of a server that's not actually doing HTTP. It's ending up passing back a status code of 200, misleading gabbi, which is none the wiser and trusts urllib3.

I tried the following against your `nc` example:

```python
>>> import urllib3
>>> http = urllib3.PoolManager()
>>> r = http.request('GET', 'http://localhost:8000/')
>>> r.status
200
```

If we turn on urllib3 debuggin for that, it seems to be an issue in httplib (which urllib3) is calling:

```
DEBUG:urllib3.connectionpool:Starting new HTTP connection (5): localhost
DEBUG:urllib3.connectionpool:http://localhost:8000 "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 None
```

That's in the `_make_request` method of `HTTPConnectionPool` in `urllib3/connectionpool.py`

And that makes it look like it is a problem within httplib's `HTTPConnection` class.

It's very late for me, so I need to not dig into this further tonight (although I'm very curious). If you have an opportunity to chase things down deeper into httplib, that woud be helpful.

I'm not sure how is best to proceed here. Gabbi makes the assumption that protocol layer is working and isn't really designed for testing that. But on the other hand, this situation has come up and is clearly very misleading. But on yet another hand, if the issue is deep in httplib, I'm not sure how to fix things, as errors in stdlib stay around for a long time.

How did you initially discover this? Presumably you weren't doing exactly as you describe above. Instead you ran into the problem and then came up with a minimal test case. Knowing the context may help suss out what to do.

In any case, thanks very much for the detailed report with an easy replication scenario.


/cc @FND simply because he'd likely find this both interesting and infuriating.

I was talking with someone about the first issue you raised and I thought: This would be a great time to start a FAQ. So keep em coming, these will all make fine faq starters.

In this particular case the honest answer is "dunno". I've not encountered a situation where I've tried to name a test and it hasn't worked, but that is probably because I'm biased in what I'm naming tests.

The full test name is the combination of what's in test_loader_name, the name of the yaml file and the the name attribute in each test (with spaces turned into underscores), and is, strictly speaking, a Python class.

To enable concurrent process grouping regex in `.testr.conf` (or other ways of doing it for other multi-process runners) to work out okay it is a good idea to not use `_` in the name of the yaml file.

I've not proved out the limits of the test names. Probably something hypothesis would help with. Some casual testing in `gabbi-run` suggest that hyphen and parens are okay.

Do you think there should be defined restrictions? My attitude so far is that if you do something wrong, as long as it tells you very early in the process and with a reasonable error message, then it's okay.

> this function

Which one? The one that's been changed? I'm not sure at this stage of processing there's any other option than imperative. We have a value, we want to to replace_template on it, we then want to turn it into a number (or fail). This does exactly that.

(replace_template itself is pretty messy, but you should have seen it in very early versions...it's actually gotten better)

The tests are very hand wavey, they prove that the right result is happening, but don't necessarily provide clear insight into the process whereby that result is caused. It's certainly not a unit test by any stretch. But I couldn't be bothered.

I was just thinking about the problem of knowing which Curio calls are indeed synchronous and which are not, with the first hat @njsmith mentioned on.

I think formalizing the traps the way it was done helps, but it does not help with curio's end user facing coroutines for which traps used are an implementation detail. For instance, nowhere does it say in the docs that `current_task` is synchronous and just gives back information without releasing control of execution.

The fact that there's no differences in calling conventions between potentially blocking and synchronous coroutines bothers me a bit, as I would say it negates in part the advantages in using async/await (as compared to threads) as underlined in [Unyielding](https://glyph.twistedmatrix.com/2014/02/unyielding.html). I feel that, ideally, it should be obvious which end-user facing coroutines only use synchronous traps and are guaranteed in the future to do so.

At the minimum, I guess those for which it is guaranteed should be documented as such, which they are not right now.


I also have a crazier idea, that seems unlikely to be good, but still sharing.
Right now, most coroutines are blocking, and most async context managers don't block in `__aenter__`. Maybe curio could make the guarantee that its own async context managers won't block in __aenter__, and all coroutines that should not block (and that do not already have an equivalent async context manager) should be converted to context managers?

Here is a list of coroutines that should be guaranteed to be synchronous, and that would need to be converted, as far as I can tell:

* `current_task`
* `timeout_after` and `timeout_at` (already working as a context manager)
* `ignore_after` and `ignore_at` (already working as a context manager)
* `clock`

But a solution should be found for the async context managers that *do* block in __aenter__:

* `AsyncFile` in the case where they are used with `fileobj = None` (if I understand correctly, this is a private interface used only when using `aopen`, so this could be factored out)
* `Lock`, `Semaphore`, `RLock`, 
* `wait`
* Everything that uses `_contextadapt` (but that should be user provided code, if I'm not mistaken... it may be a problem in code that uses `abide`)

This seems to be especially problematic for Locks and Semaphores, where context managers are the preferred interface, and there does not seem to be any good solution there.

I think in general it’s just a pain to use the library if the type of object you get from a calculation changes depending on the operation. It means you have to start doing manual casting or `hasattr(obj, ‘units’)` all over your code.

generally you want to define as `FULL_NAME = <derived or dimensionality> = ABBREVIATED` so you might want to define `percent` like this:
```
ureg.define('percent = 0.01*count = %')
```
This way it realizes that `percent` is 1/100 of the base dimensionless unit so it can be added with other dimensionless quantities correctly and by putting `%` where the abbreviated notation goes using `~` string formatting correctly displays it:
```
x = 50 * ureg.percent
print(format(x,"~")) # 50 %
print(x + 1) #1.5 dimensionless
```

That would amount to a 10x mapper, which is distinct from typical short-read mapping. The complication lies in that different genomic regions may be associated with the same barcode. You have to identify these "read cloud" first and then do mapping. Reads in these cloud have no specific order, either.

In general, the 10x mapper has room for improvement. One particular problem is that it uses bwa-mem, which is not good at all mappings. Minimap2 has the potential, but it will take significant efforts to implement. I think someone from the Bonnie Berger group has a better mapper. I thought it was on bioRxiv but could not find it.

Conversely, `fit_result.value(a)` should be whatever value is found in fitting. `fit_results.params` should be a mapping to the resulting values (the outcome of the fit) for sake of convenience. 
I personally don't really care about `fit_result.params.a`. It might be clearer to deprecate this syntax altogether in order to emphasize the difference between `a.value` and `fit_result.value(a)`.

Note that InteractiveGuess (PR #33) does `for param in model.params: param.value = new_value`. It would be nice if that didn't break ;-)
This would mean that model.params is a(n) (ordered)dict which maps parameter objects to their values. This should (of course) be the same kind of object as fit_result.params; but I guess that has to be a {p.name: p.value} mapping...
Now for the bonus question: should model.params refer to the original parameter objects which have their initial guesses as values, or should it refer to the parameter objects that are the result from fitting? I vote for the first.

I must be honest here and say that I'm not entirely sure what you're trying to achieve.
If you want to put a list inside the addict I'd just do:
```Python
my_query.query.filtered.filter.bool['must'] = [1, 2, 3] # Or any other list
```
If you want to put a list inside `.bool.must` and another one in `.bool.should` you should do that on two lines:
```Python
my_query.query.filtered.filter.bool.must = [1, 2, 3]
my_query.query.filtered.filter.bool.should = [4, 5, 6]
```

The `['must']`-syntax achieves the same thing as `.must`, brackets (`[]`) indicate item access, while the dot-notation (`.`) indicates attribute access.  Both try to access or set the same elements in addict.

It doesn't matter that they are smaller than they were trained on.  What's going to happen if you train on big objects is that the detector will find an appropriately sized version of the object in the image pyramid and train on that.  What is "appropriate" depends on the size of the detection window you configured.  With a very small dataset you might just get unlucky and the best pyramid layer corresponds to the objects being slightly larger (since there isn't an infinite gradation of sizes in the pyramid).  

In any case, more training data is the solution.

* * * * * security unlock-keychain -p 'MYPASS' /Users/me/Library/Keychains/login.keychain && /usr/local/bin/python -c 'import keyring; keyring.get_keyring().keychain = "/Users/me/Library/Keychains/login.keychain"; print(keyring.get_password("foo", "bar"))' >> /Users/me/fake.log 2>&1

That's correct. RFC 7468 says that a key in PEM format should be in the following format:

```
...
textualmsg = preeb *WSP eol
               *eolWSP
               base64text
               posteb *WSP [eol]

  preeb      = "-----BEGIN " label "-----"
  posteb     = "-----END " label "-----"
...
```

What this says is that the first line of the key is expected to contain "preeb", possibly some whitespace, and a newline. "preeb" is the `-----BEGIN...` label. The definition for "base64text" also includes a newline (not shown above for brevity) so that a parser can detect the presence of the "posteb" `-----END...`.

In other words, you are correct. The newlines are important and the key is not valid without them.

Howdy! @wbond mentioned this was a thing in IRC, and probably didn't realise that I'm the current urllib3 lead maintainer, so didn't think to ask me what would be needed.

The relevant example is [this contrib module](https://github.com/shazow/urllib3/blob/master/urllib3/contrib/pyopenssl.py). It shims PyOpenSSL into an interface that looks a lot like the standard library's ssl module. urllib3 currently codes to that interface, so that's the interface you need to shim into.

The specific requirements are:

- You must provide an `SSLContext` object that takes one argument (the equivalent of Python's SSLv23_METHOD). You can probably get away with ignoring this argument for libraries that don't have anything meaningfully similar to it.
    - This `SSLContext` object must have a `wrap_socket` method that acts as a TLS socket factory. This needs to match the API the PyOpenSSL context provides. Note that you can assume that `server_side` is always `False`, `do_handshake_on_connect` is always `True`, and `suppress_ragged_eofs` is always `True`, as we do not ever set those differently.
    - You must tolerate both blocking and non-blocking sockets. You must accept timeouts in all I/O parameters.
    - Supporting loading certificate locations is *optional*: if you only want to support the system certs, without adding custom trust roots, you can choose to do so by causing `load_verify_locations` to throw an exception.
    - Supporting client certificates is *optional*: if you don't want to support them, you can cause `load_cert_chain` to throw an exception.
    - `set_ciphers` is mandatory. Annoyingly, this API uses an OpenSSL cipher string, so you'll probably need to include a translation. Happily, urllib3 uses a very restricted OpenSSL cipher string in regular use, though our users can and do customise them.
    - `set_default_verify_paths` must be implemented, but needn't do anything.
    - `verify_mode` must be implemented, and must accept `ssl.CERT_NONE` and `ssl.CERT_REQUIRED`. Supporting `ssl.CERT_OPTIONAL` is, ironically, optional.
    - `options` must be implemented, and must accept OpenSSL option flags. Again, urllib3 mostly uses a restricted subset here, so you can probably avoid supporting many OpenSSL option flags.
- You also need a socket wrapper.
    - This needs to support the weird file-object wrapping that Python's SSL sockets do. That means you'll need to duplicate `_decref_socketios`, `_reuse`, `_drop`, and `makefile` from the PyOpenSSL wrapper.
    - You need to support `recv`, `recv_into`, `settimeout`, `sendall`, `shutdown`, and `close` (though we never use `shutdown`).
    - You need to support `getpeercert`, which must be able to support both providing the *binary* form of the peer certificate and the wacky Python-data-structure parsed form. See what PyOpenSSL does here for guidance. You will need to be able to provide *at least* the common name of the subject *and* the subject alternative name field in a form that the Python `match_hostname` function can handle.
    - For future-proofing, you should also implement `send` and `setblocking`. Both of these are likely to be used in urllib3 v2, which is being actively developed.
- Finally, you need two monkeypatching functions, one that injects your code into urllib3 and one that removes it. These should look like the PyOpenSSL contrib module's one.

Assuming you do all of these things, everything should just work. If you start working on this in a concrete way, please let me know: I'm happy to do code review and testing, and if it gets into a good shape I'd also be happy to merge a contrib module into urllib3. That would make it possible for Requests to use it.

@stefangordon @jbagot found out the problem.

After some investigation, it impacts only files recorded with Python 2.7. Because starting PyYAML 4.x, the methods “load/safe_load” becomes respectively “danger_load/load” (i.e. by default creating Python object from YAML parsing is disabled).
In Py 2.7, YAML contains this:
```yaml
body: {string: !!python/unicode 'stuff'.....
```

Which will not be parsed anymore by default (since it creates a Python object and [is vulnerable to attack](https://blog.talosintelligence.com/2017/09/vulnerability-spotlight-yaml-remote.html)). 

This means that with PyYAML 3.x series, if a malicious person introduce bad python code in the recordings, each time the recordings are replayed the code is executed (?!?!??). And you see [here](https://github.com/yaml/pyyaml/issues/5#issuecomment-260671692), this could simple as:
```yaml
favorite_activity: !!python/object/apply:os.system ['rm *']
```

I'm not sure if it means that VCRPy is not compatible anymore with Py2.7 if PyYAML is installed, or if this means that PyYAML should be pinned to 3.12 on Py2.7 and >=4.0 on Py3. Or if you just document as PyYAML did that playing recording is vulnerable to attack if PyYAML is < 4.0.

Still not sure if that sort of lazy access is a good pattern, but it's probably not harmful. I would guess there are precious few callable cache instances that don't return caches.

你的这个虽然能commit，但是with退出时并没有等待commit提的到数据库完成，但这时立刻又会把链接放入链接池，并发稍高就会立刻被另一个请求使用，mysql协议是不支持同时两个request的。

为了尽可能提高性能，链接池管理是栈式的，最后放入的将会最先选择被下一次使用。

Hi calandoa,

I've now taken the code and merged it to 'master'. I've made quite a few changes to the Python API, so you may need to change any code you have depending on it.

In summary:
* There's a new class ScanEntry which encapsulates all the useful information in an advertising report
* The Scanner class now has a 'delegate' object instead of a callback, for consistency with the Peripheral class. The delegate has a method which is passed the ScanEntry object.
* You can use a ScanEntry when constructing a Peripheral (so the address, address type, and interface number are passed across).
* A lot of the useful decoding/formatting code in test/scan.py has now been moved to ScanEntry.
* Handling of permissions problems when opening the management interface is better; bluepy-helper.c returns an error code and btle.py throws a new exception.

I've also made the code work with Python 3.x, as a lot of bluepy users have this.

Thanks again for doing this, I know a lot of people have been waiting for scanning support.

Cheers
Ian

At the time of this writing, there is no specialized function (though I'll add one in the next week or two).  So the way to go for now is to just use quaternion multiplication.  For example, if you have a 3-vector `v` (as a list or numpy array, for example) you can convert it to a quaternion as
```python
V = quaternion.quaternion(0, *v)
```
Then you can rotate it as
```python
Vprime = R * V * R.conj()
```
[If you're not sure if `R` will be precisely normalized, you might want to use `R.inv()` instead of `R.conj()`.]  You can then get just the vector components as a numpy array with
```python
Vprime.vec
```

This, of course, is relatively slow because the operation count is higher than it needs to be (32 multiplies and 24 adds).  I can cut the operation count almost in half (18 multiplies and 12 adds) by using a specialized function.  I'll leave this open until I implement that.

I'm against adding the intermediate certificate automatically to the signed certificate.

I'm using the `acme-tiny` script in the [debops.pki Ansible role](https://docs.debops.org/en/latest/ansible/roles/ansible-pki/docs/acme-integration.html) as an intermediary to request Let's Encrypt certificates. This Ansible role supports other Certificate Authorities besides the ACME CA to allow automatic switch between them if necessary. Because different applications use the X.509 certificates and private keys in different ways (some require cert, intermediate and key in 1 file, some require them in 3 separate files, and so on), role manages different parts of the certificate chain in different files and offers combined variants to other applications (you can check the linked documentation page for explanation).

Adding the intermediate certificate to the signed certificate automatically would disrupt the whole process. Yes, I know that there are ways to work around it by parsing the output files and splitting them to separate parts again, but it's an overhead for the users of `acme-tiny`. On the other hand, combining separate signed and intermediate certificate when needed is very easy to do.

Alternatively, this could be implemented as an optional argument for the `acme-tiny` script to specify a separate file for intermediate CA certificate. But since there might be more than one intermediates, I feel that this is best left for the management application like `debops.pki` to deal with, which can be programmed to recursively parse the certificates and download intermediate certificate chain as needed.

When you say "extra production settings for Gzip" does that mean that the `STATICFILES_STORAGE` setting isn't set by default but depends on some flag being enabled? If so, that's probably the source of the problem. `STATICFILES_STORAGE` needs to be set at the point when Heroku compiles your application slug, as that's when `collectstatic` gets run.

My advice would be to set `STATICFILES_STORAGE` as normal in `settings.py` for both production and development. There's no reason not to have it enabled in development.

This isn't a great example because it converts to jpg and hard-codes the filename. Also it won't work with ``image/x-imgur-album``. You might be able to come up with something better yourself. If you do, please share it here and we can improve the mailcap examples.

### Idea - simply add context argument to every implementation of http method calls

**Implementation:**
Implementation would require modifying every HTTP method handler in the `resource.mixins` module. Example:
```python
class ListMixin(BaseMixin):
    ...

    def on_get(self, req, resp, handler=None, **kwargs):
        kwargs['context'] = req.context

        self.handle(
            handler or self.list, req, resp, **kwargs
        )
``` 

As the effect developer may use three methods to provide custom per-request context variables:
* using HTTP method overrides

  ```python
  class MyResource(ListAPI):
      def on_get(req, resp, **kwargs):
          req.context['session'] = Session()
          super().on_get(req, resp, **kwargs)

      def list(self, params, meta, context, **kwargs):
          # context object content available here
          ...
  ```
* using middleware

  ```python
  class SessionMiddleware:
      def process_resource(self, req, resp, resource, params):
          req.context['session'] = Session()
  ```

* using hooks:

  ```python
  def create_session(req, resp, resource, params):
      req.context['session'] = Session()

  @falcon.before(create_session)
  class MyResource(ListAPI):
      def on_get(req, resp, **kwargs):
          req.context['session'] = Session()
          super().on_get(req, resp, **kwargs)

      def list(self, params, meta, context, **kwargs):
          # context object content available here
          ...
  ```

Pros of this approach are:
* simple implementation
* low risk of breaking backwards incompatibility
* compatible with existing techniques of providing context in falcon
* compatible with any existing falcon extension built using hooks or middleware

Also if we improve support for falcon hooks (per-method falcon hooks with `falcon.before`) this solution will still work as expected. 

**Backward compatibility concerns:**

Good practice is to allow passing any set of keyword arguments to resource manipulation methods using generic and basic resources in graceful. Anyway we cannot be sure that every developer uses this approach. We should expect that graceful users sometimes do following:

```python

class MyResourceList(ListAPI):
    def list(params, meta):
        pass

# or

class MyResourceItem(RetrieveAPI):
    def retrieve(params, meta, object_id):  # <= object id from URI template
        pass
```

We could ensure backwards compatibility by using class keyword arguments:

```python
class MyResourceItemWithCtx(RetrieveAPI, with_context=True):
    def retrieve(params, meta, context, **kwargs):
        pass

class MyResourceItemWithoutCtx(RetrieveAPI, with_context=False):
    def retrieve(params, meta, **kwargs):
        pass
```

This is feature of Python 3 but we do not target anything below Python 3.3. Of course default for `with_context` for `0.x` branch would be `False` and for `1.x` would be `True`

I want to address a concern I have about that `-1` thing... This might be a superfluous feature because we can achieve the same goal with...
```
thunderbird_todos {
    format = '\?if=incompleted_index=1 {title}'
    sort = ['time_created', True]
}
```
instead... and is probably more efficient too. This cuts down 10~ lines too.

The only reason we should keep this is if we knew the users will need both `index=1` and `index=-1`... which is not likely so it'd be good to skip `-1` right away and reverse-sort the list.

For every `index` we add in this (I'm not adding `completed_index` on purpose as it's probably not wanted anyhow...), we would have to add `-1` for that... and it goes on.

I'm bringing this up right now because there are other modules that could take advantage of this `sort` config... such as `taskwarrior`, `transmission` (sorted by percents)... `coin_market` (sorted by names, price, currency, etc). Really, many modules can use this feature... so I'd like to avoid `-1` early on. What do you think?

Theme images are very small. For example, if you have a 1500x1500 pixel photo, it has **10000 times** more pixels than a 15x15 icon typically found in a theme. In the end I decided to remove RPKI-Light support from ARouteServer.

After some tests and lines of code I noticed that current versions of BIRD (1.6.3) and OpenBGPD (OpenBSD 6.0) do not implement RFC8097 communities. OpenBGPD does not allow to use them at all while BIRD, which allows to configure "custom" extended communities, treats them strictly according to RFC4360:

> If a route has a non-transitivity extended community, then before advertising the route across the Autonomous System boundary the community SHOULD be removed from the route.

To keep RPKI-Light support in ARouteServer would mean to possibly have something "broken" and incomplete going in production; moreover, as I already said, I don't like the idea behind RPKI-Light too much.
That said, currently I prefer to keep a strict approch until the community reaches a consensus and BGP speakers include support for RFC8097 (or whatever will be eventually decided to be used to signal validity state to route server clients). If/when the draft will become an RFC, the consensus reached and all benefits/drawbacks weighted up I will take it into account and merge it again into this tool, doing my best to provide high flexibility in configuration.

In the meanwhile, I implemented some configuration customization mechanisms that allow operators to use ARouteServer to generate configs and, at the same time, to code their own policy about RPKI validation results handling within .local files (site-specific custom snippets of config). Among other things, this will allows operators to do something similar to RPKI-Light. These new features (BIRD hooks and .local files) can be found in the dev branch and will be included in the next release of ARouteServer.

What you could do is to nominate particular elements which should always generate lists, e.g.

~~~
xmltodict.parse(xml, force_list=set('child'))
~~~

This is the approach taken by Perl's XML::Simple with the [ForceArray](http://search.cpan.org/~grantm/XML-Simple-2.20/lib/XML/Simple.pm#ForceArray_=>_[_names_]_#_in_-_important) setting.

A problem with this is that if the `<children>` element is empty, it's not going to give you an empty list of `{"child": []}`. This is due to how xmltodict groups elements at a lower level, i.e. it's not `children` which is a list, but `child` which is a list.

So to work with xmltodict, I think you need to specify the enclosing element as well:

~~~
xmltodict.parse(xml, force_list={'children': 'child'})
~~~

Implies: "if you see a `<children>` element then always build a `<child>` with empty list underneath it".  Any actual `<child>` elements will then be added to that list.

Aside: there is a related but different problem, which is dealing with a heterogenous sequence of repeating elements. For example:

~~~
<stuff>
  <foo>.1.</foo>
  <bar>.2.</bar>
  <bar>.3.</bar>
  <foo>.4.</foo>
  <bar>.5.</bar>
</stuff>
~~~

At the moment you get:

~~~
OrderedDict([(u'stuff', OrderedDict([(u'foo', [u'.1.', u'.4.']), (u'bar', [u'.2.', u'.3.', u'.5.'])]))])
~~~

which loses the ordering relationship between the `foo` and `bar` elements.

This would have to be dealt with differently. For example:

~~~
xmltodict.parse(xml, force_list={'stuff': True})
~~~

could mean that `<stuff>` is forced to have a list of children, and that this list should contain repeated elements rather than grouped elements.  I don't know how practical that is to implement though.  And it would probably have implications for unparse.

That's exactly how you would do it, except the record input has to be a list arg as before, ie `w.record(*rsfi.record)`. 

Writing records and shapes now has a 1-to-1 mapping in terms of how one would read records and shapes.  

| Type | Reading | Writing |
| --- | --- | --- | 
| Records | `r.record(...)` | `w.record(...)` | 
| Shapes | `r.shape(...)` | `w.shape(...)` |

What's more, the new `.shape()` method can also take a GeoJSON dictionary if you prefer that. 

The new way of writing shapes is explained in the "Adding from an existing Shape object" section of the Readme: 

> Finally, geometry can be added by passing an existing "Shape" object to the "shape" method. This can be particularly useful for copying from one file to another:
> 
> ```
> >>> r = shapefile.Reader('shapefiles/test/polygon')
> 
> >>> w = shapefile.Writer()
> >>> w.fields = r.fields[1:] # skip first deletion field
> 
> >>> for shaperec in r.iterShapeRecords():
> ...     w.record(*shaperec.record)
> ...     w.shape(shaperec.shape)
> 
> >>> w.save('shapefiles/test/copy')
> ```

@ljvmiranda921, that sounds nice! When I was mulling over this for a while, I had two ideas which are somewhat intertwined:

1. **A `neighbor_history` attribute:** We could insert the neighbours for every iteration into a `neighbor_history` attribute in the base class. This would just mean that we have to access the `neighbor_idx` array in the `topology.compute_gbest()` method. This would enable us to analyse how the neighbours change over time.

2. **A new feature for the `plotters` module:** Visualise the neighbours by drawing a line between two neighbours in the animation. To avoid drawing the lines twice, we can omit all the indices in an array of neighbours that are lower than the current index of the particle. This might become a bit messy for dynamic topologies but we could default it to `False`.

I added a modified version of @lahwaacz's `get_ancestors` function and a `get_parent` as well. We can solve your original question like this:

```python
>>> import mwparserfromhell as mw
>>> tree = mw.parse('prefix {{foo|http://example.com}} bar')
>>> link = tree.filter_external_links()[0]
>>> tree.get_parent(link)
'{{foo|http://example.com}}'
```

Here is a fixed version of my `find_containing_template` function (probably still a little awkward but it does the job):

```python
def find_containing_template(code, link, names):
    """Return the template matching *names* in *code* that contains *link*."""
    for node in reversed(code.get_ancestors(link)):
        if isinstance(node, mwparserfromhell.nodes.Template) and node.name.matches(names):
            return node
    return None
```

Hope this helps.

from __future__ import division

def get_cat_rate(group, request):
    cats = Cat.objects.filter(name__startswith="Tom").count()
    limit = 3600 // cats
    return (limit, 3600) # 3600 seconds = 1 hour

@ratelimit(key='user_or_ip', rate=get_cat_rate)
def myview(request):
    pass
```

If I understand you correctly, you have a large test suite and you only want to run a subset of the tests.  But some of the selected tests depend on other tests that are not in the selection, so these tests get skipped, because their dependencies have not been run.  What I could imagine would be to add a global option `ignore_unknown_dependency`.  If set to `False`, the default, a test will be skipped unless all the dependencies have been run successful.  This is the current behavior.  But if this option is set to `True`, a test will be skipped if any of the dependencies has been skipped or failed.  E.g. this would ignore any dependencies that have not been run at all.  Would that suite your use case?

Looks like you'll want to extend all Validators that could ever encounter a File object to actually support validation on them. I'm under the impression that both the HTTP and Swagger1.2 specs only support them in form-encoded bodies. If that's the case, you can just change the form validator to some extended version of the `Swagger12ParamValidator` it currently uses and make that extended validator support File objects.

That said, once we get 2.0 support and delegate all validation to bravado-core, this feature needs to be supported there not in pyramid_swagger. Up to you whether doing this work is worth it, and I'd suggest making sure bravado-core supports the same feature too.

Hey @TeaPearce, this is expected in practice. The hazard function is an unconstrained linear model, which could increase or decrease, and due to sampling/estimation errors, could become negative! If this happens, the survival function will increase. 

In a perfect world, with infinite data, this would not happen. But of course, with finite data, this will happen in practice. There's not much you can do about it - maybe try to stay in the hazard space where for analysis? Or, replace the negative values to be 0 instead.

Regarding (i like this word, BTW) `Event`: IIRC most synchronization primitives implemented by `dummy_threading` only work as much as is needed to make the code run somehow. For example, i think `dummy_threading.Lock().acquire()` does not actually wait until a `release()` has been called.
I just noticed that there is no real reason of using an `Event` anyway. The original idea was to reduce network overhead by using a `Event` implemented by the manager instead of an proxy of the `Event`, but then i used `treading.Event` instead. The `if stopevent.is_set()` check in `evaluate()` is for the case that some other thread (like a GUI) called stop. In this case, the slave nodes would stop evaluating, but the `evaluate()` call would still try to wait for the results. The check exits in case this happened.

If you literally want to pass extra data to `context` for use in the template, you can subclass the `RegistrationView` you are using and add the appropriate data in `get_context_data`.

For example:

```python
class CustomRegistrationView(RegistrationView):
    def get_context_data(self, **kwargs):
        context = super(RegistrationView, self).get_context_data(**kwargs)
        # add your additional data here
        context['foo'] = 'bar'
        return context
```

I'm gonna be offline for most of the next week so I just wanted to note that 'consolidated_updates' means "consolidating the expiration updates of existing redis keys" - it's not a consolidation of all the tickets/prs.  

I wanted to work on some of the auto-expiry stuff too that someone mentioned on another ticket but I didn't think it is worth it yet.  Redis doesn't currently have a command that can run a single lookup for a key+value, so there would need to be 2 O(1) queries and lookups per session hit to support that -- and possibly another to update.  The overhead of 2 guaranteed gets + 1 potential set isn't worth the utility of that feature.  I've logged a feature request to Redis to do everything at once, and am lobbying their devs.  

If Redis will support a consolidated GET for value + expiry, the next feature I'd target is to add a configurable "float" for EXPIRE renewals.  The general idea is that if you have a 1200s expiry window and a 10s float, EXPIRE is only renewed if the session is more than 1190s old. In practice, if a page loaded a total of 10 ajax calls and assets locked behind a session, the float would result in only 1 expire command -- the package just wouldn't bother to hit redis for the other 9 (or subsequent pageviews for a bit).

I think that's just due to how epochs are implemented in `InputMode.Spark`.  Since Spark doesn't have a notion of repeating RDDs, we actually implement `epochs` as a Spark UnionRDD of `epochs` copies of the RDD.  This, combined with the fact that Spark doesn't support early termination of an `RDD.mapPartitions()` operation (used to feed data to TF), means that the entire UnionRDD must be completed "processed" in order for Spark to successfully complete the operation.  So, in this mode, even though the TF side has "completed" by `steps`, the Spark side must still "process" the entire dataset... it just does so by consuming the iterator but skipping the feeding part.

So basically, you should try to match your `steps` to your `epochs` fairly closely to minimize this overhead.  As an alternative, you can use `InputMode.TENSORFLOW` and just use TensorFlow's native support for reading files from HDFS directly.

No worries, I think I've resolved it.  I was about to cancel the report. Thanks for the prompt interest anyway.   It was a strange one to do with caching and how Daphne must behave when denying requests.

IP Address of virtual adapter was not in ALLOWED_HOSTS.  Django was not complaining about that.

The client (I tried Firefox/IE/Edge/Chrome - all the same) had received half of the file and then cached it. Daphne must somehow half serve the file.  Server was responding 304, so it was using the (broken) cached version (Phaser is 3.5Mb).

Adding correct IP to ALLOWED_HOSTS fixed it.

There are three more issues related to `topk()` that may or may not be worth considering right now. I don't want to complicate this performance issue with unrelated API issues, but some of these might be easy to address, so I thought I would list them here all in one place:

- [The documentation for `topk()`](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/ndarray/ndarray.html#mxnet.ndarray.topk) doesn't specify whether the returned `k` elements are sorted. They currently are (because of the underlying reliance on `sort()`), but we don't know if they will be afterwards. Could the documentation clarify this? ([See our issue on this here](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/api/python/ndarray/ndarray.html#mxnet.ndarray.topk))
- We actually care about the locations of the top `k` items as well as their scores. We therefore have to flatten the array, call MXNet `topk`(), and then use numpy's `unravel_index()` to map the indices back into the shape of original array. It would be really great if we could pass in the unflattened tensor, along with the axis to perform `topk()` on, and get directly back indices in the shape of the original tensor.
- Finally, we currently call `topk()` multiple times on contiguous slices of our batched tensor ([see the loop over `sentno` here](https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye/blob/335124558e33538543e1574edba755e9458ea506/sockeye/inference.py#L1285-L1290)). We could get a lot of performance improvement across all architectures for batched decoding if the `topk()` call could run on multiple subregions of a tensor in parallel.

What are left:

1. An efficient multi-process loader
I notice there is already one single process loader. `MXBox` has a multi-thread loader, however due to the GIL of python, multi-thread does not help much when loading large image. Meanwhile, fetching large data from queue will cause overhead in multi-process. The best solution I have seen so far is the `pin_memory` in `pytorch.Tensor` -- which passes a reference between processes instead of copying. 

2. CIFAR100 / SVHN  more dataesets

3. `watchman version`

> there isn't a reasonable way for any tooling to automatically know when the user wants the watch to go away

I disagree. For the user to unwatch a root, they would need to use whichever Watchman client sent the `watch-project` command.

The idea is that the Watchman client acts as the layer between the user and Watchman, completely isolating the two. So the client would send both `watch-project` and `watch-del` commands to maintain its unique `watch-list`. Thus, the client would need to provide a means for the user to watch/unwatch a root.

The unique `watch-list` lets Watchman clients avoid stepping on each other.

p.a.event  in Plone 4 used to store the timezone in an extra field while having the datetime itself 'naive' (w/o timezone). This was a bad idea and involved tons of postprocessing when accessing the values. 
In Plone 5 p.a.event stores the datetime with the correct timezone (non-naive) and theres no extra field for the timezone any longer.

It looks like while migration from 4 to 5 the timezone was dropped and set to UTC. 

Solution: Write a script that iterates over all events, read start and end, check if timezone is UTC and if so, correct the timezone (and probably the hour) and write back.

Setting LINGER = 1 is telling zmq, "Please discard messages immediately when I close."

Since you aren't holding onto references to the sockets you create (FD and underlying are integers), they are getting garbage collected in each loop, which means they are being closed immediately after sending, starting the LINGER timer for discarding messages. Since you have set LINGER = 1, that gives the socket at most 1ms before discarding messages that haven't been delivered. Setting linger to a larger value, e.g. 1000 for 1s, will result in all messages being delivered.

The last example is working, not because of the sleep, but because you added `socks.append(sock)`, which means the sockets are no longer being closed. LINGER has no effect in this case, because the sockets stay open until the end.

@jsmitchell thank you for raising this issue. I have had this exact same issue and found this after about 2 days worth of trying to figure out why pyzmq was hanging. I was using 17.0.0b1 and downgrading to 16.0.2 fixed the issues I was having.

While is around 300 lines of code I have included a [gist](https://gist.github.com/costrouc/44b5dbcd7783c9b0fb3a95f065f577d4) that can reproduce the issue. I was implementing an asyncio version of the Majordomo Protocol. The issue non deterministically occurs when the scheduler sends all the tasks to workers (last message). The socket then appears to freeze. Workers do not receive the messages sent by the scheduler and the scheduler does not receive heartbeats from the workers. Since the workers do not receive a heartbeat response they assume the scheduler is dead and disconnect then reconnect. Then the schedulers sees some messages were lost and the sends them back to workers (cycle continues).

Here is a quick hack. Please modify accordingly.
```
import tensorflow as tf


def rename(checkpoint_dir,dry_run=True):
    checkpoint = tf.train.get_checkpoint_state(checkpoint_dir)
    with tf.Session() as sess:
        for var_name, _ in tf.contrib.framework.list_variables(checkpoint_dir):
            # Load the variable
            var = tf.contrib.framework.load_variable(checkpoint_dir, var_name)

            # Set the new name
            new_name = var_name
            if 'biases' in new_name:
                new_name = new_name.replace("biases", "bias")
            if 'weights' in new_name:
                new_name = new_name.replace("weights", "kernel")

            if dry_run:
                print('%s would be renamed to %s.' % (var_name, new_name))
            else:
                print('Renaming %s to %s.' % (var_name, new_name))
                # Rename the variable
                var = tf.Variable(var, name=new_name)

        if not dry_run:
            # Save the variables
            saver = tf.train.Saver()
            sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
            saver.save(sess, checkpoint_dir+'new-model-ckpt')

# Usage
model_dir = "/path/to/older_version_ckpts"
rename(model_dir,dry_run=False)
```

About the pre-release process:
Lets say you want to release v0.2.0 in about 2 weeks and most/all of the features are integrated. Then you can create a new git tag and mark it as prerelease on Github (for example 0.2.0-rc1). All maintainers can now test this fixed version and report bugs. They all test the same version, not some random commit on the master branch. This means if the same bugs occur for mutliple people it might be easier to find, and also you can compare against the next RC2 then.

More investigation has given more insight to the problem. 

The error is caused by the `client.run_task` call on line 182. I am receiving a failure with the reason "RESOURCE:MEMORY" when there are not enough available resources for the task. The failures fails to populate the `self.task_ids` and the error above is shown.

In my case, and I suspect in other cases as well, this behaviour is not ideal. For the purposes of my pipeline I need the task to wait until resources are available and then to submit the task_run again. I have inherited the ECSTask class and overwritten the run function in order to allow the response to be wrapped like so:

` def run_task_on_ecs(self):


        # Submit the task to AWS ECS and get assigned task ID        
        overrides = self.__get_overrides__()

        valid_reasons = ['RESOURCE:MEMORY',]

        sleep_time = 20
        task_started = False
        wait = False

        while not task_started:
            response = CLIENT.run_task(taskDefinition=self.task_def_arn,
                                       overrides=overrides,
                                       cluster=self.cluster)

            if len(response['failures']) == 1:
                failure = response['failures'][0]
                if failure['reason'] in valid_reasons:
                    LOGGER.debug(
                        'ECS task status for tasks %s: %s' % \
                            (self.task_def_arn, 'WAITING'))

                    time.sleep(sleep_time)
                else:
                    raise RuntimeError(
                        'Unkown reason for ECS run_task failure ')
            elif len(response['failures']) > 1:
                raise RuntimeError(
                    ('Multiple failure reasons when trying to '
                     'run a task on the ecs cluster.'))

            else:
                self._task_ids = [task['taskArn'] for task in response['tasks']]
                self.remote_task_started = True
                task_started = True`

OK guys, I've written a production ready, and hopefully useful to the community, asyncio extension to botocore. The library is currently included in https://github.com/quantmind/pulsar-cloud in the [asyncbotocore](https://github.com/quantmind/pulsar-cloud/tree/master/cloud/asyncbotocore) module but it is self-contained and could be striped out if needed.

The API is the same as botocore but with the addition of ``http_session`` keyword which is an optional ``asyncio`` compatible HTTP client (it must expose the ``_loop`` attribute).
As far as I know there are two of such clients:
* [pulsar](https://github.com/quantmind/pulsar) http client
* [aiohttp](https://github.com/KeepSafe/aiohttp) http client (@asvetlov does aihttp client/session have the ``_loop`` attribute?)

If used with [pulsar](https://github.com/quantmind/pulsar), the library can also use greenlet in an implicit asynchronous fashion. Check https://github.com/quantmind/pulsar-cloud for more info

Feedbacks welcome!

My hypothesis...

So pygit2 is installed system wide, the cffi extension should be built and installed, and probably it is. But when importing it is not found, so it tries to generate it at runtime. Since all this happens as a regular user, without write access, you get an error.

The name of the cffi extension is defined by https://github.com/libgit2/pygit2/blob/master/pygit2/_utils.py#L75 and depends on a number of variables, like the version of cffi. This means for instance that the binary package in your distribution needs to be updated every time there is a new version of cffi.

For context you may check issue #441

One possible solution would be to reduce the number of variables the name of the cffi extension depends
on.

But then there is #529 ... I have to check how the new method works.

> I use dict/hash to find nodes by SHA1 but they are not internal to the graph. Each vertex is simply a list of lists, except for commits which are just a binsha. The dict containing tree vertices is discarded at the end, as only the blob vertices need to be found by binsha.

Our data-structures are actually similar! Even though yours seems to store a little less - I for example keep everything I see during traversal, and thus can lookup everything by sha. The reverse is also true, and the SHA can be obtained for every index. That might be an opportunity to safe a few hundred megabytes, but won't speed up the operation.
That one optimization you do I will certainly try to apply again, now that I rolled back my version of it the program works as before - 9.5GB resident size is what it takes and by now 21min 30s when using the cache.

```
time echo | git-reconstruct --cache-path lut.bincode.lz4  $PWD ../linux-stable-workingtree

Loading graph...
Ticked 61319 blob bits in 7505212535 commits
unimplemented
echo  0.00s user 0.00s system 34% cpu 0.002 total
git-reconstruct --cache-path lut.bincode.lz4 $PWD ../linux-stable-workingtree  1125.54s user 19.59s system 89% cpu 21:26.05 total
```

Even though the graph lookup algorithm itself might be suboptimal, I have a feeling that any improvement will likely cost more memory than there is. But I am open to be corrected on that one :D!
One improvement I see possible is to parallelise the blob-to-commit lookup - it's a perfect opportunity for huge gains.

FWIW, I don't think it's a good idea that running a SeleniumLibrary method in Python automatically would trigger the run-on-failure functionality. Especially if SL is not used in combination with Robot, it could have really strange side-effects. In other words, I think the current behavior is better than the old one. 

This kind of backwards-incompatible change should have obviously been mentioned in SL 3.0 release notes, but I guess nobody, including users testing the preview releases, noticed it. If there would have been complaints then, we could have considered this, but now I'd say that ship has sailed.

Those who want the old behavior have at least these possibilities:

1. Instead of using SeleniumLibrary methods directly, use them via the `run_keyword` method that Robot nowadays uses when it's calling SL keywords. For example, instead of using `sl.title_should_be('Expected')`, you can use `sl.run_keyword('title_should_be', ['Expected'])`. For more details about the `run_keyword` method study Robot's [dynamic library API](http://robotframework.org/robotframework/latest/RobotFrameworkUserGuide.html#dynamic-library-api).

2. Implement the functionality yourself. You can decorate your own keywords or SeleniumLibrary methods with a decorator that does it automatically like in Selenium2Library 1.8.

3. Construct your library using the dynamic API and implement the run-on-failure functionality in `run_keyword` similarly as SL 3 and newer.

In my opinion, No, it would not justify raising the minimum selenium version. The reason for not raising the minimum, in general, is to not force people to install only the latest. Not knowing the individual and particular circumstances our end users are in, it gives them the freedom to self choice what works for them. There could be very legitimate reason unbeknownst to us for setting the version at which they choose.

A good criteria or reason for bumping the minimum version of selenium would be if the change in our library without a change in the minimum selenium version would cause an import error for all users. Yes, if one wants to use Edge then one needs some minimum version of selenium but not everyone needs or uses Edge. One might even say its an edge case. (Sorry, couldn't resist). I would much rather see some code that checks selenium version or existence of webdriver.edge and errors out if not there then to raise the minimum version.

- Travis configuration changes should get their own PR. I don't know too much about Travis and canot merge PRs touching it without knowing what change do.

- As I have commented earlier, I would like to see separate PRs fixing individual Python 3 compatibility issues without affecting Python 2 compatibility. Two easy topics to start are:

  - Exceptions: `except Exception, err` -> `except Exception as err`.

The issue with making it the default is that it needs to be set on your model rather than on the rating and we wouldn't want to change the default ordering behind the scenes as this would likely be confusing. 

You can set this as the default ordering for your model by adding it to the meta class though. Something like this should do the trick:

```
class MyModel(models.Model):
    class Meta:
        ordering = ('-ratings_average',  )
```

The docs are here https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/models/options/#ordering

If re-resolving dependencies results in a lower version being put in the lockfile, `sync` installs that one. That’s the whole point of pipenv. Once it’s out of the lockfile, if you didn’t call `clean` or `uninstall`, pipenv isn’t managing it. We don’t assume we are managing every package in the environment all the time (see `—system` or note that pipenv can be installed and used inside a virtualenv). 

So does pipenv refuse to downgrade something that was explicitly resolved to a lower version? No. That is not a secret action. It is a strict pin in the lockfile. Things in the lockfile always get installed in the environment. Just imagine as if we iterate over the list of things we determined need to be installed and for each of those we `pip install` it (because this is exactly what happens).

It may not have been obvious to you, but I would be interested in seeing an example of dependency management software that behaves the way you expect pipenv to behave.

`—system` is not compatible with most other flags. Pipenv requires an isolated environment to do resolution. The flag that does what you want in combination is `—system —deploy`. Please see the docs for more info. We have improved error messaging in the next release.

That seems to contradict the [blog post](https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/) you mentioned. If I got it right, the post explicitly says that having URLs in setup.py is a bad practice because developers that want to fork and modify one or more of the dependent libraries would also have to modify setup.py in order to remove the URL and thus making it an abstract dependency (only name + version, but no location). This leads me to the assumption that the right way to have dependencies for example on GitHub or somewhere else and not on pypi is to have a requirements.txt (pip) or Pipfile (pipenv) which include the specific locations of the dependencies or use `--find-links=` to specify the location for the dependencies and have setup.py contain the abstract dependencies, i.e. include package name and version number but omit the location where they can be found.

@gsalsero you can actually put `azure` in `[dev-packages]` and `azure-mgmt-compute` in `[packages]` and this should allow you to generate a lockfile:

```ini
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]
azure-mgmt-compute = "==3.0.1"

[dev-packages]
azure = "*"

[requires]
python_version = "3.6"
```

This is a nifty workaround that I usually recommend, since pipenv will always let `packages` win conflicts when they are encountered.

Thanks for the question, @rueberger, and apologies for the slow response. In theory, a producer instance should never *silently* fail to deliver a message. Any message delivery failures should have some explicit signal associated with them. In some cases, this signal will be a thread exception that triggers a certain number of retries, after which it is raised to the main thread with the intention of notifying client code. Though this is the intent, bugs in the producer have in the past and probably still result in the "silent" failure you're seeing, in which a thread exception either never gets retried or does not get raised to the main thread. In these cases, `delivery_reports=True` can be used in combination with a method similar to  the one in the pykafka README to ensure that all messages were delivered successfully and keep track of any that weren't.

In the case of the synchronous producer, delivery reports are checked repeatedly until a [timeout](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/b1bf00778d3f6458d99ddc14bb5ebf9bbeee9fab/pykafka/producer.py#L380). This timeout logic was [recently added](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/releases/tag/2.8.0-dev.1), so past versions behave differently. It was added to fix a situation where synchronous production would hang indefinitely after a delivery failure.

Hopefully this sheds a bit of light on your issue. If you have any additional information to give that might make it easier to pin down precisely what you're hitting, that would be helpful.

This bug happened when there were more messages to consume than the `max_bytes` value supplied in a fetch request. Kafka returned exactly `max_bytes`, cutting off a message in the middle. Thus, the buffer it returned ended up being smaller than the reported length of the message set by the size difference between the length of the message set and `max_bytes`.

This was fixed [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/commit/ac5fda0d310a6a7f59a9c1b06a36244de99c92aa).

The concrete suggestion would be something like this. (I'm following the format on the API spec https://help.airbrake.io/kb/api-2/notifier-api-version-23)

> /notice/error/backtrace/line/var
> Optional. A list of variables and their value within a line of code in the backtrace. Each var element contains the printed value of variable, and requires the @name attribute, which is the name of the variable. Optional, @type, indicates the class or type of the variable.

For example, imagine you had the following Python script:
```
def foo():
    my_var = 123
    raise Exception
```

Here's the backtrace portion of the XML sent to airbrake...
```
<line file="path/to/foo.py" number="3" method="foo">
  <var name="my_var" type="int">123</var>
</line>
```

Thanks @scottpurdy ! I rebased on the newest code, and resolved some prev. merge hiccups hopefully. For a short summary, this PR consists of: 
* `MetricSubmetric` - which is just a helper code allowing to clean up some other metrics.
* `MetricModelCallback` - the main code here, allowing to get in instance of active model in a metric, used to "hack" additional info for the metric.
* `GlobalStore` - a helper, wrapper around a global list. I've read your comment somewhere else and agree - this is up for a discussion. 
 * Pros:
   * some prints/debug around the global list
   * user just needs to know there's `utils.GlobalStore with get()/set()`
   * also all code is wrapped just in one class logic - when we want to get rid of this functionality, we'll just remove all mentions of GlobalStore, no problems finding global variables with custom names.
 * Cons: 
   * additional cruft code - agreed the added functionality is not something that cannot be really handeled by just an array. 

So yeah, GlobalStore has questionable and I will easily replace it with just some global variable. I just find the functionality less intrusive when packed into 1 simple code.

Ok, these messages are funky. They've got some weird edge cases and their bi-directionality is problematic. My proposal is that we:

1. Remove MSG_SETTINGS and replace it with a one-way MSG_SETTINGS_WRITE. This is always from the host to the device and has a payload of values to write.
2. MSG_SETTINGS_SAVE is unchanged
3. MSG_SETTINGS_READ_BY_INDEX gets replaced with MSG_SETTINGS_READ_HOST and MSG_SETTINGS_READ_DEVICE (the READ_DEVICE message takes READ_BY_INDEX's message id). READ_DEVICE always sends a string (no NULL).

Will put together some changes in sbp and firmware for this. Hope this will get us towards making these stable.

I looked a bit more into the problem, and actually it's somewhat more complicated than initial thought.

First problem is that ``session`` variable name is already a [Flask global variable](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/templating/#standard-context). Hence we should fix the loop:

```jinja
{% for session in sessions %} -> {% for s in sessions %}
```

The second and real problem here is that when you sign up, the session is not saved to the database ``accounts_user_session_activity`` table.

Issue regarding copying of words/text is a Android OS issue, and happens with non Kivy applications like email clients. This can be fixed by:

    Unticking "Next Word Prediction".
OR
    Switching off word completion.

Both cannot be switched on.
What happens is it goes back in your word prediction as you press space, it then enters that prediction automatically in random order or it just duplicates your text and mixes it with predictions.
Here is links to the same issue (Not kivy related)

​http://forum.swype.com/showthread.php?12403-Duplicating-words-after-pressing-space-amp-backspace
​https://www.phonearena.com/news/SwiftKey-glitch-causes-double-trouble-for-Android-users-after-update-UPDATE_id70887

Alright, I've given this some thought about how to implement it now. What we have is a profile with an analytic form in Fourier space, but no analytic form in real space (aside from of course the Fourier transform integral). I can think of a couple ways to implement this. In both ways, implement in the C++ layer (like SBExponential.h/.cpp) and use the analytic form for kValue and fillKValue.

1. For xValue, perform the Fourier integral each time the method is called to calculate for that point. For fillXValue, perform an FFT on an array of k values.

2. Store a lookup table of x values (if fillXValue is called first, store a copy of the matrix made here, otherwise create one with an FFT of k values), and interpolate when xValue is called.

Option 2 will be much faster (but less accurate) if there are frequent calls to xValue. The question is, how often is xValue called? I suspect it isn't that often, and so for most cases, we'd want to err on the side of better accuracy at a slower speed. What are your thoughts on this, Rachel?

The implementation of  ```galsim.utilities.interleave``` as it is now, relies on the user giving the list of images in the right order of offsets. The default ordering is a little unnatural and can be confusing for the user. Moreover, such errors from the user's side cannot be caught through unit tests. Thus, it is recommended that the user provide a list of offsets corresponding to each of the images in the list.

dupe #510

> any info on how Dave could fix this?

You need the proper hinting set up to get the font to render at subpixel position. Iirc if hinting is not present, then both DirectWrite and GDI ClearType will default to snapping towards the nearest whole pixel. Afaict this is what FontAwesome 4.2+ is currently doing.

Browsers rendering with DirectWrite have a hardcoded behavior to skip hinting tables altogether and render at full subpixel precision for font-sizes greater than 20 pixels. The rotation hack forces them to do the same with font-sizes of 20 pixels or smaller. However, that does not fix the issue for browsers stuck with GDI ClearType, which iirc does not have the same behavior of starting to render at full subpixel precision.

I don't think there's a way around it: you *must* have proper hinting tables to fix this completely.
The good news is that having TTFAutoHint generate hinting works OK for most icons, atleast given a cursory glance.

We built two test scripts to try and narrow down the leak and to determine whether the problem was in boto3 or a result of how boto3 was being called by s4cmd.

The first script calls boto3's put_object() directly.  The second imports BotoClient from s4cmd.

In both cases, we ran the scripts against the same 1,000 and 2,500 file corpuses that were causing leak with s4cmd.  Neither script leaked, so it appears to be a cycle that's being created within s4cmd somehow, not an outright bug in boto3.

Two scripts follow:

#!/bin/env python

# native boto3 calls

import boto3
import botocore
import os

testdir = '/tmp/testdir'
client = boto3.client('s3')

files = os.listdir(testdir)

for file in files:
    client.put_object(ACL='private', Body=open('%s/%s' % (testdir, file), 'rb'), Bucket="wawd-s4cmd-test-bucket", Key="%s/%s" % ("testdir", file))


-----

#!/bin/env python

# s4cmd BotoClient calls

from s4cmd import BotoClient
import os

testdir = '/tmp/testdir'

client = BotoClient({})

files = os.listdir(testdir)

for file in files:
    client.put_object(ACL='private', Body=open('%s/%s' % (testdir, file), 'rb'), Bucket="wawd-s4cmd-test-bucket", Key="%s/%s" % ("testdir", file))

> Is there any problem with defining RESOLWE_EXECUTOR_COMMAND on per machine base?

So the problem is defining `RESOLWE_EXECUTOR_COMMAND` with the incorrect executor. For example, for `local` it has to be `/bin/bash` (and not `sudo docker` or similar), while for `docker` it has to be a proper way to execute the docker command (with or without sudo).

So the purge test doesn't care if it is run with docker or with the local executor. It works either way. But the executor that is used must be configured correctly.

If I understand correctly, the docker test requires the docker executor? Perhaps the suggestion by @tjanez with having a per-executor command configuration (one for local, another one for docker) would be best.

> Just curious, do you know why Python 3 deprecated xrange?

In Python 3 the `range` function is Python 2's `xrange` function and so the old name was dropped.  They are both generators which can be iterated over.  In Python 2, the `range` function returned a list, to get this behavior in Python 3 you need to explicitly call `list` on the range object, `list(range(10))`.  Why exactly this change was made is a bit beyond me.  I think it was intended to simplify and clarify the language.  Having two function that do nearly the same thing but in a slightly different manner is a bit confusing.

The current (and intended) case in makeotf is that script and language statements affect only the enclosing feature, and are not attached to lookups defined within a feature. As a result, script and language statements used within a named lookup have no effect on other features which reference the named lookup. I find this confusing and difficult to explain, but am reluctant to break existing font data. After thinking a bit more, I propose leaving the wording as is, and just add a warning to makeotf when script and language statements are used within a named lookup.

I am arguing from the perspective the two kinds of docs are aimed at different classes of users, who need different tone / format:

- Well-structured narrative docs with good examples are aimed at users who are new to the library, and need the "guided tour" approach which the examples support.  They focus on the "main path" / common case, and expose the new user to concepts in an order which aids them in building an understanding of why to use various features, more than the nitty-gritty details.  In Sphinx, the narrative docs should always contain links to the appropriate sections of the reference docs.
- API reference docs are most useful for refreshing an experienced user's memory of an API's precise signature / contract:  conciseness / precision is most helpful for them.   Building explicit references from the reference docs is tricky / confusing, because the narrative docs may have multiple locations which discuss a given API.

For an extensive example of documents built using these principals, see Pyramid's [narrative docs](http://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/pyramid/en/latest/#narrative-documentation) versus its [API reference docs](http://docs.pylonsproject.org/projects/pyramid/en/latest/api/index.html).

> I still maintain that we are adding more concepts / classes to gain functionality which we already have.

Functionality -- yes. It is certainly possible to do these things with what we have.

Usability (aka, not repeating ourselves) -- I don't think so. It looks like we're typing connection far too often when we need to be specific about it, right?

As @tseaver notes, GoogleCloudPlatform is an interesting case, as gcloud-python itself doesn't count as supporting Python 2.6 for free in my view (it's a developer facing component of a commercial service offered by Google), but the arguments in my post may apply to some of gcloud-python's dependencies (such as pycrypto).

Maintaining Python 2.6 compatibility is also getting more difficult with time, as there are a number of nice features that originally landed in Python 3.1 (like set literals, set and dictionary comprehensions, field autonumbering in str.format, collections.Counter and collections.OrderedDict) that were included in the original Python 2.7.0 release. and hence folks first learning Python with either Python 2.7 or any current version of 3.x are likely to take them for granted. collections.OrderedDict itself is actually available in RHEL 6.5+, but it's pretty easy to stumble over the others (for anyone that doesn't default to using printf-style string formatting, the lack of str.format field autonumbering is a particularly easy Python 2.6 incompatibility to hit)

Assuming that the main reason for retaining Python 2.6 compatibility is RHEL & CentOS 6, then a more fruitful path forward may be to encourage affected users that can't upgrade to RHEL or CentOS 7 to look at the softwarecollections.org project and the related Red Hat Software Collections component included in RHEL subscriptions. If folks don't want to invest time in upgrading the foundational layers of their stacks, then they can't reasonably expect to indefinitely keep upgrading higher level third party components.

How many reduce shards are you running? Each mapper is going to write data out for each reduce shard, and each output file is going to have an associated buffer. So if you have 20 reduce shards, and the buffer size is set to 2MB (I believe that is the default) you could end up with theoretically up to 80MB in just buffers (It does double buffering for performance). That could be really hard to fit in a 128MB VM. (Particularly if the scheduler decides to run multiple requests in the same instance.)

@FcrbPeter Calling `PublisherClient.publish` adds to a batch, but doesn't necessarily commit that batch. The batch will be committed in the background after the default [`max_latency==0.05s`][1] has elapsed. If you'd like to commit the batch immediately you could do something like:

```python
from google.cloud.pubsub_v1 import types

message = types.PubsubMessage(data=data)
batch = publisher.batch(topic_path, message)
batch.publish(message)
batch.commit()
```

Hi @rtibbles , when the platform is idle for 10 minutes or the browser is closed and reopened after 10 minutes, the issue is that the Logout is not consistent across the learners. 
We are getting the following results:
1. Sometime we get the error page.
2.Sometime the learner is logged out and the learner is not indicated that his/her account has been logged out. 
3. Sometime the learner is not logged out.
4. Sometime the learner is not logged but after when they continue they get the error page.

Since the logout is not consistent and as there are so many outcomes, it becomes very challenging for the teacher to give directions to get the learners on task and begin the class. 

If you feel there aren't any quick fixes, then we would need to go ahead implement and see how it fairs out in classrooms.

Thought of two things that might move us forward:

> Regarding variable naming, this is probably the best chance we have to establish a consistent settings API.
> 
> If the cost is a few one-time special cases to handle existing environment variables, IMO it's worth it.

I would personally love to see `KOLIBRI_HOME` renamed to `KOLIBRI_DATA_DIR`, as it's more representative of what goes there (what makes a home a home?), and because it matches the KOLIBRI_DATA directory name we use when we export/import to a drive. Just thinking through the implications, however, so we're not assuming it's going to be simple:
- There are dozens of references to `KOLIBRI_HOME` across the codebase, and also in plugins. 
- Some references are to the envvar, others to `conf.KOLIBRI_HOME`, others to `settings.KOLIBRI_HOME`.
- Maintaining backwards compatibility means maintaining the alias in all 3 contexts. So we'd look at both `os.environ["KOLIBRI_HOME"]` and `os.environ["KOLIBRI_DATA_DIR"]`, support both `settings.KOLIBRI_HOME` and `settings.KOLIBRI_DATA_DIR`, etc. Dev confusion can be minimized by comments in the code, but it does add some complexity.
- But yeah, better sooner than later if we're going to do it. Can you remind me *why* we're doing it again? Because now I want to do it, but I'm not sure why. :laughing:

> I think we need to have different PRs to address the four issues you point out in the brainstorm. I'm currently working on the first issue regarding occupied port. I'm wondering if I should close this PR and create a new PR for it, or just modify this PR for the change.

As for opening up other issues, perhaps opening up a general issue about doing health/sanity/configuration checks before launching kolibri is best -- then we can split out each individual issue in case we decide to pursue them separately?

> Do you mean that we not only need to inform users there are other processes using this port but also show them which processes are (possibly the PID)?

I mean this only as an option, but it's very "nice to have". As in: possibly very difficult to implement for all platforms -- and likely that it will be a cause of problems, too.. so we don't want to have error-prone error handline :) If you want to know which PID is occupying a port, you might have to dig very deep into the host platform, and it's not likely to be easy. Perhaps listing other processes labeled "kolibri" is easier.

Or perhaps just telling the user "Hey you probably have another Kolibri instance running, use `kolibri stop` and see if that works" will work for 99% of all issues :)

Hi,

The docs for `GetDataDownloader` are just in the [docstring](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads/dfp.py#L312). This doesn't look like a client library bug to me as I haven't been able to reproduce it. Feel free to reopen if you disagree but otherwise please direct your question to the [DFP API Google Group](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/google-doubleclick-for-publishers-api).

I think I now have a handle on what is going wrong here. The reason you're getting an error indicating an unexpected end of data is because... the decoder **is** reaching an unexpected end of data. The `ReportDownloader.DownloadReport` method currently doesn't take any special precautions in the event that a `StringIO` file-like object is provided--e.g. it is conceivable that any given chunk may truncate a multi-byte utf-8 character, causing the decode to fail.

As a work-around, if you provide a `BytesIO` instance and get the full report contents, you can decode that to get a string. Alternatively, you could just use the `ReportDownloader.DownloadReportAsString` method, which similarly gets the full content before decoding--worth noting, I also noticed that `DownloadReportAsString` doesn't chunk the response, meaning it could be problematic for particularly large reports.

It looks like there's a few improvements we can make here for a future release.

Regards,
Mark

Hey, I can help you with this. So to start with, it seems you have things set up enough to be connecting to you workspace server. The credentials in question are metadata id/pw (OMR). You can check those using SMC to access the metadata server. The need to exist, of course, and they need to be able to access the workspace server (app server context) you're trying to connect to.

Assuming your id and pw are valid OMR credentials, the next thing I would think would be that you may have multiple App Servers defined in metadata. If you don't specify the specific app server (like this:
```
'appserver' : 'SASApp Prod - Workspace Server'
```
then the object spawner you're connecting to will only try to use the first on in it's list. If your id doesn't have permission to use that one, it will fail. If you have multiple app servers, then you really probably want to specify which one to use.

Let's get this working this way first, specifying your credentials (it will prompt you for them). Once we figure this out, we can try to set up IWA. The error you get with this is due to the path for the spiauth.dll not being in your windows system path variable. the spiauth.dll should be part of your SAS install. You can just search for it by name with windows. It may be something like this:
C:\Program Files\SASHome\SASFoundation\9.4\core\sasext

That's documented in the 'Local' IOM section, but as you mentioned, setting sspi=True will require it to be in the path also. I haven't gotten to update the doc for this yet. Of course, if you add this to the path then IWA may work, but if it uses the same credentials as you were specifying, you will likely get the same error. 

Can you check on what appservers you have, if your credentials are valid for which of them, and try specifying the one you want (assuming you have more than one. 

Thanks,
Tom
