This is not documented ( but should ). You can find many example in the ```./qa/conf folder```, please look at the ```flow*``` files.

The parsing engine is generic so all the data are parsed the same way. So if you look any test for port range validation, you can apply the same syntax to packet-length.

The "comment" for the class in master is :
```
class ParseFlowMatch (Section):
	definition = [
		'source 10.0.0.0/24',
		'source ::1/128/0',
		'destination 10.0.1.0/24',
		'port 25',
		'source-port >1024',
		'destination-port [ =80 =3128 >8080&<8088 ]',
		'packet-length [ >200&<300 >400&<500 ]',
		'tcp-flags [ 0x20+0x8+0x1 #name-here ]  # to check',
		'(ipv4 only) protocol [ udp tcp ]',
		'(ipv4 only) fragment [ not-a-fragment dont-fragment is-fragment first-fragment last-fragment ]',
		'(ipv6 only) next-header [ udp tcp ]',
		'(ipv6 only) flow-label >100&<2000',
		'(ipv6 only) icmp-type 35  # to check',
		'(ipv6 only) icmp-code 55  # to check',
	]
```

A space can be see as a way to ```or``` the tests defined.
So ``` >200&<300 >400&<500``` matches a number between 201 and 299 or 401 and 499.

For numeric operators, you can use ``` = > >= < <= ``` 

(master only - not in the 3.4 branch) 

For binary operators, you can use ``` = ! ``` 

```=``` will force an exact bit match when not using it will force a check for the bit. I have not written any test case for this I was told the code generated by  ```= first-fragment&= dont-fragment``` and ``` first-fragment&dont-fragment ``` is correct and did what the user which reported this issue expected.
I never had to use it so I am unsure of how it matches packets on the wire.

Looking at the code, TCP virtual ports can't have a Cookie persist type, hence the JSON message error.  I can see a number of ways of skinning this cat and ending up with a cute hat instead of bloodied hands:
- Only HTTP-type protocols can have cookies
- Only TCP protocols support any kind of session ID
- Everything else is UDP and we only get Source/Dest IP as persistence types.

Knowing all this, we have a limited number of protocols we can use with cookies, a smaller subset that allows using a property of the TCP connection, and everything else is UDP.  It might even be better to create an inverse ruleset that specifies the types of session persistence that _cannot_ be used with that type... that seems like it would require less information to make the correct decision,

The main issue here is that each use of the provision script will create a *new* Deis cluster. The Azure template will create a new private network for each cluster. Even if you use the script once per account, the hosts will be unable to communicate with each other.

The scenario you're describing is unsupported by using our provision scripts. 

> Extending this argument, could we use three machines one from digital ocean, one from AWS and the last one from Azure to create a cluster? If not, what is preventing from this being possible(discovery url?). I am only asking this to make sure I understand what you are saying.

Yes, the main requirement being that all of the hosts can communicate over a private network. This is something that you'd have to configure manually. Additionally, latency between hosts is an issue. If they are on different networks in different locations, you're likely going to run into issues.

In short, this topology probably isn't worth it. If you're trying to save money, perhaps deploying to Digital Ocean is a better bet.

> When we create a cluster, can all the machines be of different sizes as long as they meet the minimum system requirements?

Yes.

But we absolutely need to give at least a 10% price bump when estimating the price for the user.

It seems we didn't test this adequately. Calling `tdl.parse()` with a filename argument leads to the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "tt.py", line 3, in <module>
    list(tdl.parse('/home/goodmami/grammars/erg-trunk/lexicon.tdl'))
  File "/home/goodmami/repos/pydelphin/delphin/tdl.py", line 265, in _parse
    for line_no, event, data in lex(f):
  File "/home/goodmami/repos/pydelphin/delphin/tdl.py", line 201, in lex
    lines = enumerate(stream)
ValueError: I/O operation on closed file
```

The bug is a bit sneaky:

```python
def parse(f):
    if hasattr(f, 'read'):
        return _parse(f)
    else:
        with open(f) as fh:
            return _parse(fh)
```

The `_parse()` function returns a generator which doesn't evaluate until it is iterated over, but the `else` case returns the generator instead of yielding its values, so the file gets closed as soon as the function returns (i.e., before anything is parsed). There are several ways around this, but one of the nicer ones (`yield from`) is not available until we remove Python 2 support.

Finally i solved it by myself.
The problem is there in Environment variables 
Suppose you are installing per user or for all users the environment variable called PATH has "C:\Program Files\xxxx". Here the Program Files has the space that why it is not able to decode. The only thing that you need to do is create one more variable PROGRAMFILES with value C:\Program Files and use %PROGRAMFILES% where ever program files is used.
PATH (user or system)variable should not contain any space.

Observing the development of the python ecosystem for some years, this is one of the most important features and conda is - IMHO - the only project with the potential to bridge this feature gap to ecosystems such as in php (composer), ruby (bundler) or java (gradle).
[Composer schema](https://getcomposer.org/doc/04-schema.md) is a good inspiration for what a dependency manager can include. Rethinking this could also help creating a simpler way for build tools.

While pip is talking about "requirements" and conda about "environments" I'd propose to use a clear terminology for the aspect of describing desired dependencies (requirements) and the aspect of locking and sharing a fixed and solved dependency list (environment): maybe s.th. like "environment.yml" and "environment.yml.lock" (in analogy to pyproject.toml and .lock) or "requirements.yml" and "environment.yml" in analogy to existing files.

Having backwards-compatibility is a very important thing, as well. Supporting the same workflow and description format as used by now must be ensured to meet slower project cycles. Nevertheless it would make sense to give a more expressive format enough thought to support similar expressiveness than existing description files (not just a list of dependencies).
Providing features to import from files such as requirements.txt and Pipfile (see https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/4205) could also draw on people working with those into using conda.

If such a workflow would be designed the right way I would not see any necessity for files like pyproject.toml (see https://github.com/pypa/pip/pull/4144 or https://github.com/sdispater/poetry) because conda - as a more general package manager - is way better suited for this job.

There's no `--force` flag that applies here.  I'm pretty close to getting rid of the `--force` flag completely (or hiding it in a deep dark corner).  The problem is that `--force` is overloaded and means different things in different contexts, but often those different contexts all apply during a single conda operation.  So now in master there are for example `--force-reinstall`, `--force-remove`, and `--clobber` flags.

The flow in this PR is that if you do `conda create`, and the environment already exists, we prompt to ask you if you want to delete the environment.  The default answer is 'no' of course.

The way this works (or should work, anyway) is that python is built with the new compiler.  Python is all C, so that's fine as far as ABI conflicts go.  Python bakes its compiler variables and such into a _sysconfigdata_something.py file.  We copy that to _sysconfigdata_$HOST.py, and replace it with a _sysconfigdata.py that we retrieved from python with an older compiler.  This is effectively a fallback.  If you need to support the old compiler, just don't set ``_PYTHON_SYSCONFIGDATA_NAME`` - that should be all.  It's more challenging to use the new compilers, but hopefully we'll take care of that by setting up the env var for you in activation scripts.

B in cigar strings is a backwards skip - like a negative N operator.

It was added for certain instrument types (I forget which now. Complete genomics maybe?) that worked on oligo/primer sequencing with the possibility of oligos being overlapping.   That gives a small stutter in the sequence, which can be compensated for by a negative skip to rewind a few bases on the reference.

In practice it was never supported well as it causes lots of headaches for algorithms like pileup, plus IIRC the instrument manufacturer had a tool to output merged data that removed the B ops anyway.

I'd just expunge it and pretend it never existed. :-)

Hi folks, because I am fixing related #6928, I will also address this one as the potential fix will be the same. Here are my ideas:

1. kubectl.exe will be copied to user home folder, say “c:\users\jon\\.azure-kubectl”. Right now copying to “program files” is not ideal as it is still not in search PATH and also doing it requires elevation.
2. After download, CLI will check whether it is in OS's PATH env variable. If not (likely first time download),  CLI will emit out the following warning text:
```
Please add "C:\Users\jon\.azure-kubectl" to your search PATH so the `kubectl.exe` can be found. 2 options:
    1. Run "set PATH=%PATH%;C:\Users\jon\.azure-kubectl" or "$env:path += 'C:\Users\jon\.azure-kubectl'" for PowerShell. 
       It is good for the current command session.
    2. Update system PATH environment variable by following "Control Panel->System->Advanced->Environment Variables",
       and re-open the command window. It is once for all.
```
If you do the 2nd, then CLI will never warn any more.
Letting CLI update the PATH is doable by tweaking the PATH's registry value. I am not so sure whether we need to go that far, also, users still need to reopen the command window unless CLI broadcasts it which is a native Win/API invocation. But anyway, if you really like CLI to do this, please let me know.

In general, we avoid these sort of checks on the client for future compatibility. Say the service started allowing 10 ACLs (theoretically) and we had a check in the library to only allow 5. We'd not only not allow valid requests, but we'd also make it impossible to update since if they got a 10 ACL list, modified it, and then tried to put it, it wouldn't work. Basically, putting it in our SDK has the exact same issue as you hard coding it in your application.

In this case after evaluating the error message we'll take a temporary fix to the client until the server updates it's message. Once that's released, we'll likely remove this.

As of [0.32.0](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/releases/tag/v0.32.0) the library supports automatic calculation of transactional MD5s for upload and download. Transactional MD5 is more versatile than stored MD5 as it is computed by the server on the actual stored data. It does not have the issue where the stored content MD5 can get out of date as the blob is updated. Simply use the validate_content MD5 to enable this for the upload of any blob type and blob download. Note that using MD5 validation has a performance penalty and HTTPS already protects against bitflips on the wire so this feature should be used primarily if HTTP is used.

The library does not build in calculation of the total blob MD5 or validate it on download as this historically in other libraries causes issues with the stored MD5 being out of date with the content. However, the user may still set and retrieve this value using the content_settings. Note that by default stored MD5 not returned by the download APIs unless particular provisions are made as described in the get method docs. It can still be retrieved by getting the blob properties.

A race condition was discovered with solving islands in parallel if kinematic objects are present. If a kinematic object is part of 2 or more islands, and the islands are running in separate threads, the `companionId` field of the kinematic body will get stomped on in `btSequentialImpulseConstraintSolver::getOrInitSolverBody`.

Basically the solver is storing it's own private data in an object that is being shared between threads. That can't work. If each body had a unique-id assigned to it, then the solver could store the data it wants in an array indexed by the body's unique-id.

looks cool, glad I have it running but I meant something a bit different. which could potentially make pybullet scene calculations and image generation 10x faster than if they were implemented the way numpy is. Tensorflow happens to be used for machine learning, but really it is just a math library that creates a graph of mathematical operations saved in a protobuf format that can be run much faster than the python interpreter. 

This alternative design is one where pybullet could supply tensorflow ops to retrieve and set transforms, load segmentation images, rendered images, do quaternion math, etc. In other words the images might never need to be copied from c to python.

Essentially, use the ability to [implement new ops](https://www.tensorflow.org/extend/adding_an_op) and add [custom data readers](https://www.tensorflow.org/extend/new_data_formats) to define functions under the namespace `pybullet.tf`.

example:
```python
import tensorflow as tf
# new pybullet tensorflow functionality
import pybullet.tf as pbtf 

# op that when run returns true if the simulation is running,
# false otherwise.
simulation_running_op = pbtf.SimulationIsRunning()

# this returns a tensorflow op, not a numpy array
camera_op = pbtf.getCameraImage(width,height)

# can have some configuration params for how it works...
random_camera_op = pbtf.randomizeCameraPose(camera_op)

# this is some user defined function 
# that loads some regular tf model
# and weights from disk, let's say it
# is predicting segmentation
model_tf_op = my_load_model(random_camera_op)

segmentation_op = pbtf.getCameraSegmentation(random_camera_op)

# some train function
training_op = my_train_fn(model_tf_op, segmentation_op)

with tf.Session() as sess:
  train_with_pybullet = tf.while_loop(training_op, simulation_running_op)
  # probably needs some time limit parameter or something
  sess.run(train_with_pybullet)  

```

Additional ops can exist for getting transforms between objects, creating and configuring qp solvers, basically any functionality the user wants, but they get a huge performance boost from it being tensorflow ops instead of directly running python code.

we need only two timeouts:
1. connect timeout (waiting to connect to endpoint)
2. read timeout (waiting for data from endpoint)

also to completely get rid of our monkey patching:
3. ability to set socket timeout for a given response (comes from botocore requirement)

for 1) I think this doesn't fix it because it seems like it still includes the time for waiting for a connector from the pool.  We achieve this here: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore/blob/master/aiobotocore/endpoint.py#L219 by wrapping a CeilTimeout around the `_create_connection` call.  This is still heavy handed and instead should behave like requests: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#timeouts, that is ideally this timeout would be eventually passed to `socket.settimeout` by the call `loop.create_connection`.  It's rather unfortunate that `loop.create_connection` does not take a timeout parameter as you really want the underlying socket class to do this...as otherwise if you block your main loop you could trigger the timeout w/o the server actually trying to connect to the server.

for 2) we achieve this via wrapping the `ResponseHandler._wait` method as well as the `.read` method.  It seems like a tighter coupling would be clearer than what's presented in this PR, no?  Ideally around the time the request for data is made, and the time it is received.  I think if there were a tighter coupling one wouldn't need as many changes.

for 3) we currently do this via: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore/blob/master/aiobotocore/endpoint.py#L137 by calling `response._protocol.set_timeout`.  So it would be nice if there was a public method for this.

I think this is the problem https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/http_parser.py#L327

if server tries to upgrade connection to http/2, it sends "connection: upgrade" and "upgrade: h2" headers. aiohttp in this case mark connection as upgraded and tries to use custom parser (i.e. websocket)
but according to spec it should not do this and just need to continue to use http/1.1

Document wide input hiding is supported in nbconvert. 

I am still working out how to implement _filtering_ based on tags, but that's not togglable hiding. 

Hiding can be done based on tags and templates, but it will only be possible in certain formats, such as HTML. There is a prototype of how to do that using only CSS at https://github.com/mpacer/hiding_tags_nbconvert

There are issues with how to avoid jitter with hiding large sections of text. There was an issue on nbviewer about toggling input visibility, but not anything that was merged. That was partially because of the jitter issue partially because it would only act globally which wouldn't fit the use case of hiding some cells and not others. @parente can speak more to that than I can re: nbviewer on this issue. 

I do not believe the plan was for nbconvert to look at the cell metadata to determine if something will be hidden/filtered on output. If people do want that feature it might be nice to have a way to manually "persist" the states with a custom tag stem assigned to the relevant cells (tag+"_input"; tag+"_output"). Then if we want that tag could be stored in the notebook level metadata & nbconvert can watch for that. Then nbconvert would add that value to the appropriate traitlet value for filtering input/output/cells (a traitlet which does not exist yet).

@muneeb-ali @kantai mentions above his desire to keep the `blockstack` cli as an HTTP client for the `blockstack api` (current naming). This seems to me a good idea. It opens the door for us to deal with the components individually. This will allow for separation of concerns and make development on top of the API easier. We maintain reference implementations (`blockstack-cli`, `blockstack.js`) and the [API spec](https://blockstack.github.io/blockstack-core/) and then users can build out their own implementations as they see fit (`blockstack-golang`, `blockstack-swift`, etc..). This will reduce our development effort and allow for more extensibility. It also eliminates the need for a config file for the CLI: just set the `host` and auth credentials to connect to a different server.

I think that reducing the number of command line tools is not necessarily a good goal. If we are searching for user understanding and ease of use then having a single command line tool for each component of the architecture helps users better understanding how the system is working (i.e. when I type `blockstack` I'm a client operating on the `blockstack-api` node. The `blockstack-api` node depends on `blockstack-core`, `bitcoind` and `bitcoin-utxo` nodes). When you are communicating with multiple components via the same CLI this mental model quickly becomes muddied and causes confusion. Also more command line tools doesn't necessarily mean more mental overhead. The `blockstack-api` and `blockstack-core` tooling will just have `stop` and `start` with little else.

Another other option could be to add more documentation in the `--help` for the cli to make sure users understand what is going on. In that model we would implement `blockstack api` and `blockstack core` subcommands for spinning up the Indexer and API respectively. I think this could be good (fewer command line tools for users to remember) but would keep the current tight coupling we have between components and retard the development of other implementations. If you look at systems like Elastic, Spark,  a profusion of CLIs: 1 (just a process) or 2 (server/client like `bitcoind` and `bitcoin-cli`) per component in the system.

To summarize the discussion so far: common cases such as unpacking:

```
def f():
    return 1, 2, 3

def g():
    _, _, x = f()
    return x
```

or iteration:

```
def boink_10_times():
    for _ in range(10):
        print("boink")
```

already _do not raise errors_. The only error case seems to be deliberately assigning _just_ to `_`, such as:

```
def foo():
    _ = 42
<stdin>:2: local variable '_' is assigned to but never used
```

This doesn't seem to be a common idiom. A `for` loop _must_ have a variable. Unpacking _must_ assign each component to some name. But in this case, the choice to assign to `_` was optional and deliberate. Why wouldn't pyflakes complain about this?

The reasons for assigning to `_` mentioned so far are:

- Assigning unused function parameters to `_` to silence pylint. Satisfying pylint is not an objective for pyflakes (in fact the entire project was borne of frustration with pylint's propensity for such superfluous errors).
- Assigning the result of a function which does not return None to `_` to "be explicit" about ignoring the return value.

In Mesa terms, a *step* is a time-tick of the model, within a single instantiation. A *run* is a model being instantiated and then going through some number of steps. BatchRunner generates some number of runs, and collects the reporter values at the end of each run. Recording each step of each instantiation would probably be a good thing to add, though.

The number of runs the BatchRunner generates is proportional to the number of combinations. That's why the baseline in the example is four runs -- one for each parameter combination. Setting `iterations=5` means that BatchRunner will generate five runs for each of those combinations, for 4x5=20 runs in total. 

The reason for having this option is that since models usually have a random element, it's good practice to run a model multiple times even with the same parameters to make sure that you're seeing typical behavior.

It does look like you are cramming too much into one step.  If you look here: 

https://github.com/seifely/mesa_boxworld/blob/master/mesa_boxworld/mesa_boxworld/agents.py#L1229

you'll automatically pass that `if` check since you just found a path. That means your agent will have planned and moved in one model step. If you change that to an `elif`, then your agent should wait until the next step to make a move.

Hi,

yes it is, we had a new release 10 days ago.

This is hard for me to review because there is a bunch of changes and no rationale for any of them.

For one, I think an important goal of this library is that ordinary, non-complex usage should be the default, and complex features should be available if you want them. So, `accept_global_loop` should default to True. Also, since we already have this parameter under the name `forbid_global_loop`, I'd prefer to keep that name to not break an existing API (defaulting to False).

I like the current approach of having a special fixture named `event_loop` better than yours of inspecting fixtures for a specific type. Dependency injection based on fixture names is a usual pytest convention, but based on types is not. Fixtures can also be overriden by their names, etc.

As for changing the scope of the event loop used, I think the existing mechanism of overriding the event_loop fixture should be good for that, but it needs to be refactored a little. Currently the `event_loop` fixture is responsible for producing the event loop instance, but not for closing it (the plugin closes it); this should be refactored so the fixture itself closes the loop when done. This is more in the spirit of Pytest and enables some additional use cases, but it makes for a more complex `event_loop` fixture so the readme should be updated with a proper recipe for overriding it.

Async fixtures look interesting, those would be valuable to support.

If you want to contribute, a good first step would be to fix the default `event_loop` fixture so that it's a normal yield_fixture (function scoped) that closes itself, and remove the close from the plugin itself. Also update the readme with a good recipe for overriding the event_loop fixture, both for using a different loop and for using a different scope.

That's handy in cases where upstream dep removes one of its dependencies hence removing transitive dep.

In this case I don't think `ndg-httpsclient` or `pysan1` are explicitly necessary for the core package - they will be pulled in together with `acme` package. Ok, so here is what I think the C Pickler needs for cloudpickle to make use of it:
- a settable `dispatch` attribute for per-type save() callbacks; this takes priority over built-in code for types and functions, but not necessarily for other objects such as int or list
- an overridable `save_fallback(obj, exc)` method called when the C Pickler fails to serialize `obj`: the fallback can either implement its own serialization for `obj` or re-raise `exc`
- to allow calling the following methods (no need to make them overridable):
    - `save(obj)`
    - `write(bytes)`
    - `memoize(obj)`
    - `save_reduce(*args, obj=None)`

`save_fallback` isn't strictly necessary (we can override everything in `dispatch` as done currently), but may allow to cut down on the amount of custom code in cloudpickle, and would also ensure faster serialization where possible.

Regarding the underscores: as far as the `loom-viewer` is concerned this is not an issue, since it uses a list of potential default attributes, and supporting plain `X` and `Y` is as simple as putting them higher in the list.

I don't know about other code you are using of course, but here is the equivalent Python code:

```python

def firstMatch(keys, searchkeys):
	for key in searchkeys:
		try:
			keys.index(key)
			return key
		except:
			# not present, try next key
			continue
	return None

def firstPair(keys, pairs):
	for pair in pairs:
		x, y = pair
		try:
			keys.index(x)
			keys.index(y)
			return pair
		except:
			# one of the keys not present, try next tuple
			continue
	return None
```

test:
```python
>> pairs = [('X', 'Y'), ('A', 'B')]
>> keys = ['A', 'X',  'C', 'B',  'D', 'Y'] # list of dictionary keys, order unimportant
>> firstPair(keys, pairs)
     ('X', 'Y')
>> firstPair(keys, [('B', 'C')])
     ('B', 'C')
```

You could use it as:

```python
# First matching X/Y attribute, backwards compatible with older loom files
firstPair(ds.col_attrs.keys(), [('X', 'Y'), ('_X', '_Y'), ('SFDP_X', 'SFDP_Y'), ('_tSNE1', '_tSNE2'), ('_PCA1', '_PCA2')]
```
Maybe that would make it easier to make your scripts backwards-compatible?

REST APIs, and especially tastypie, tend not to handle file uploads well. What I usually do is write a view that accepts file uploads and stores them in a temporary location (something that can be cleaned out regularly via a cron job) and returns a path to that file within the Django storage class used. Then, to add that file to a model, you send the storage path along in a regular JSON request, and on your resource move/copy the file from the temp location to the permanent location.

Let me make some final remarks from my pov:

> We know that if a user can change something, then they can read it, that's how the Django admin does things.

Django admin uses the `change` permission because the admin interface _is_ about _changing_ objects. It makes sense there. The GET request on a REST API by definition is about _reading/viewing_.  I would think that most devs quite simply don't expect DjangoAuthorization do refuse reading based on a missing permission to change. 

> This version is more secure, because it forces the developer to think about what they're doing. 

One of tastypie's advertised [features](http://tastypieapi.org/) is to provide _resonable defaults_. Wouldn't it be quite reasonable to assume that an API's GET (by definition: read) and PUT (change) methods, being different operations in all intent and purpose, also require different permissions?

### Summary
As I understand it at the moment:
- The cfg file *must* now specify the **release**.
- Gromacs 5 
  - tools are autodetected by parsing `gmx help` — this relies on `GMXRC` having been parsed already: is this guaranteed?
  - The driver command is always `gmx`; in particular, it is not possible to use `gmx_d` or mix in e.g. `gmx:mdrun` (which currently becomes `gromacs.tools.Mdrun`) and `gmx_d:mdrun` (which becomes `gromacs.tools.Mdrun_d`).
- There are no more `gmx:toolname` entries for Gromacs 5 in the cfg file.
- Gromacs 4 tools are read from the *Gromacs.tools* section of the cfg file and are not autodetected.

### Current concerns
1. I don't know why the doc strings do not work for Gromacs 5 tools but that should be fixed so that it works. 
2. I also don't want to give up the flexibility to use single and double precision and, say, MPI binaries all in one session.
3. Is it still possible to set the tools for Gromacs 5 in the cfg file?
4. `extras` is not used often but it is convenient in some cases, so I wouldn't want to give it up unless it becomes a major headache.

### Discussion
I think we need a good idea what the config file should look like (discussion in #49). This PR suggests that we should have
* required: Gromacs release
* optional: path to `GMXRC` (for autoloading without manual sourcing of `GMXRC`)
* ... what else?

Would it be possible to break this PR in two pieces:
1. simple refactoring (functions etc) and *optional* autoloading of Gromacs 5 and 4 tools (e.g., if the `Gromacs.tools` section of the cfg file is empty or set to some magic such as `__AUTOLOAD__`.
2. changes that mess with the cfg file format (e.g., getting rid of Gromacs 5 `gmx:toolname` entries).

Come to think, one problem with this approach is that not all tools are prefixed with `g_`. For instance, `grompp`, `eneconv`, `trjconv`... should not just show up as `g_grompp`, `g_eneconv`, `g_trjconv`.

Somewhat ugly but feasible solution: create a dict with the exceptions:
```python
aliases5to4 = {
   'grompp': 'grompp',
   'eneconv': 'eneconv',
   # ...
   'sasa': 'g_sas',
   'convert_tpr': 'tpbconv',
}
try:
   alias4 = aliases4to5[name]
except KeyError:
   alias4 = "g_" + name
``` 

One could also use the reverse dict
```python
aliases4to5 = {name4: name5 for name5, name4 in aliases5to4.items()}
```

@meeuw Good idea thinking about how to get the coverage report to run when using tox. This is closely related to #6.

Here are some thoughts:
1. `coverage erase` doesn't seem to erase `.coverage.{envname}` files -- so, if tox didn't combine the coverage files the previous time (e.g. the user manually canceled tox from finishing), then it wouldn't erase all the existing coverage files. Or, if the user ran `tox -e py27` to just test Python 2.7, then `coverage erase` wouldn't erase that file the next time around.
2. This requires running two extra virtual environments, solely for the coverage report. That seems like a lot of overhead for what is essentially one command at the end (`coverage combine && coverage report`). Since we have `setup.py test -all` -- can we just add the necessary set up commands there? Then we don't have the overhead of the extra virtual environments and the time it takes to create/activate/run them.
3. If we add the coverage report code to `tasks.py`, then you could choose to run tox with or without the coverage report. E.g. `./setup.py test -ac` or `./setup.py test -a`.

Here is a command we could use to delete the appropriate coverage file via `setup.py`:
```
remove_coverage_cmd = 'rm -f $COVERAGE_FILE'
```
And, `coverage erase` could be used by the `test -all` option prior to calling tox.

I like keeping the test suite running as quick as possible, hence my preference for not adding virtual environments that aren't actually testing anything.

Tox doesn't really do a good job of supporting on-demand test customizations (e.g. running tests with or without coverage reports at the end). That's why I lean towards putting it in `tasks.py`, where we can allow the user to control it if they want.

I might be overthinking this. What do you think?

I guess it does get more complicated when you have data coming from multiple instruments and hence multiple sampling rates (potentially). I'd been envisioning that you could define an n-d array containing all the data having a common time reference, and this would avoid the problem. Unfortunately I'm not an expert on what HDF5 does internally when writing datasets to disk. I'd expect that if you can define the full dataset size up-front, it'll ensure the data isn't fragmented within the file, but if you have an unlimited dimension which gets extended then fragmentation is likely to occur. Raw disk access will also be slower if you're writing to either multiple datasets or multiple files simultaneously, because the writes are very unlikely to be to contiguous bits of disk and so the hardware/filesystem caching won't be as effective.

There will be approaches to this in software that currently stores data from instruments; it's probably worth reviewing these to see whether any are adaptable to NWB. But I maintain my inclination that NWB is best suited (and I gather designed) as an exchange format for data, not necessarily something to write directly when acquiring. I don't think it should try to do everything :)

```
class OneToOneField(fields.Field):
    parent_prop = ''
    child_prop = ''

    def __init__(self, parent_prop, child_prop, *args, **kwargs):
        self.parent_prop = parent_prop
        self.child_prop = child_prop
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def save(self, obj, data):
        if not self.readonly:
            child_obj = getattr(obj, self.parent_prop, None)
            if not child_obj:
                raise ValueError('Unable to find %s on %s' % (self.parent_prop, obj))
            setattr(child_obj, self.child_prop, self.clean(data))
            child_obj.save()
```

`  # pragma: no cover` says that this block does not have to be tested.  If it is added on a simple statement line -- just that line coverage is not demanded.  if added at a line defining a new block (such as `if`, `for`, `def` etc) -- the entire block does not have to be covered. e.g. we have already
```
$> git grep -A2 'no cover' 
duecredit/injections/injector.py:        if not self._active:  # pragma: no cover
duecredit/injections/injector.py-            lgr.error("Must have not happened, but we will survive!")
duecredit/injections/injector.py-        lgr.debug("Assigning original importer")
--
duecredit/utils.py:except:  # pragma: no cover
duecredit/utils.py-    on_debian_wheezy = False
duecredit/utils.py-
```

so in the first case -- the `if` statement including `lgr.error` invocation does not have to be tested.  In the second -- exception handling block.

It is trying to make request on MPRISv1 interface. We support only MPRISv2. (there are large differences)

> Can the missing method be added to mps-youtube in an easy way?
No.

> What would be the solution to this?
use plugin which support MPRIS2. On arch linux they have mpris2 version of plugin [0]. I suppose you could at least use their patch if you are not on arch.

[0] https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/pidgin-musictracker-mpris2/

### Why MPRIS runs as a separate process?

1. MPRIS currently bind to mpv socket to use its JSON IPC to get real playback status and send commands to mpv. As we do the same in the main process (I think mainly for displaying progress) we need to run in the separate process. (A processes cannot bind twice on the same socket.)
2. MPRIS loads some non-pythonic system libraries (glib mainly). There has been some libraries conflicts with libraries used my the main process. We need to run in the separate process to separate those incompatible libraries.

These two makes it necessary to run MPRIS as a separate process now. I quite also like that crashes in MPRIS will not cause main process to crash and playback can continue. You can argue that it should never crash, but people have so many configurations (I think we learned that from bug reports) it is easy to miss something. I have tried really hard in the last PR to ensure that MPRIS can not crash main process.

Oh, and I don't know how you are implementing the Python part, but I recommend using the mpris2 API; that way it can be separate from mpsyt and will also work with other mpris2-compatible media players.

@mgcrea , the power consumption is definitely one of the first things to look into, but not only:
the quality and length of USB3 cables matters as well. The depth module itself may be faulty.

In case the disconnects are deterministic and can be reproduced by application/code - you're welcomed to open new issue and we will definitely address it.

Otherwise you need to check the HW setup (R200+USB3 cable + USB3 hub) and probably replace some components to isolate the weakest link.
Additional hints/things to consider:
 - Are you using the original (30cm) USB3 cable supplied with R200?
 - Does this specific HW setup works/fails when connected some other PC/OS ?
 - Did you try to use Ubuntu stable (LTS) kernel instead of 4.11.0-13?

Hi @ajhyndman, if the type fields are created directly "by hand" (not using any framework like `graphene`), the order of the fields depend on the dict that you are using to hold the field names and values.

So, if you are using `{}` instead of `OrderedDict`, then the dict will be un-ordered and un-deterministic depending on which python env you run the operation in (unless you are in Python 3.6 which preserves the order of the elements in normal dicts `{}`).

Because of this fact, the schema will print one values or others.
If you want to fix this issue, then you would need to switch from `{}` to `OrderedDict` in your type resolvers (same in resolver arguments), or using a framework like Graphene that ensures this order.

Let me know if that fixes the issue (please reopen if not).

I have the same issue but I found workaround.
1. Exclude the choices property
2. Create an Enum for the choices
3. In the node, create a property with the same as the choice property and use the Enum
4. Create a resolver for the property in the node

See example below. Hope this helps

```python

class ModelWithChoices(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=10)
    choices_property = models.CharField(max_length=20, null=True, blank=True,
                                         choices=(("choice_one", "choice"),
                                                        ("other_choice", "other choice"),) )


class ChoicesPropertyEnum(graphene.Enum):
    CHOICE_ONE = "choice"
    OTHER_ONE = "other choice"


class ModelWithChoicesNode(DjangoObjectType):

    choices_property = ChoicesPropertyEnum()

    class Meta:
        model = ModelWithChoices
        interfaces = [relay.Node]
        exclude_fields = ['choices_property']

    def resolve_choices_property(self, info, **kwargs):
        return self.choices_property
```

I think right now, you have to inspect the query AST(`info.query`) to see what fields are queried. Then you can use the `load_only(*columns)` option in the sqlalchemy query.
```
fields = get_query_fields(info.query, info.fragments)
query = TypeAuthor.get_query(info=info).options(load_only(*fields))
```
or something similar. See https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/348 for ideas about how to implement `get_query_fields` and alternatives.

Of course this is a simplification, since this only works for the first level of the query. You'd actually have to also look at relationships, recursively, and apply the `load_only` to those too. It might look like this:
```
fields = get_query_fields(info.query, info.fragments)
query = TypeAuthor.get_query(info=info)\
    .options(*(
         joinedload(relation).load_only(*relation_fields) 
         if relation is not None else load_only(*fields) # for top level entity
         for relation, relation_fields in fields
))
```
but this still isn't right, it only takes care of the first level of relationships. 

The `joinedload` option specifies an eager loading method, which we also want to avoid the n+1 problem(see also https://yacine.org/2017/02/27/graphqlgraphene-sqlalchemy-and-the-n1-problem/).

http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/loading_relationships.html
http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/loading_columns.html

I think this is caused by the "ignore outliers in chart scaling" aggregating all series together to compute outliers, rather than doing it on a per-series basis.

Your "train" dataset contains orders of magnitude more data than your validation dataset, so if you look at the entire dataset in aggregate, every validation point is an outlier.

We should fix this by computing outliers on a per-series basis, to ensure that every data series is included in the plot.

The issue you describe where you see/don't see the option is likely a result of caching, where your browser was keeping a copy of an older version of TensorBoard source - so you were launching TensorBoard 54 but actually seeing TensorBoard (x<54)'s frontend. When you launched it on a new machine, it had a new address so you didn't hit the stale cache. [Should I blame caching?](shouldiblamecaching.com)

We should also put the TensorBoard version into the UI, so that it'll be clearer to the user when they are hitting a stale cache. (Maybe we should also decrease the cache time down to like a day.) I'll make another issue for that.

For background, the [Thread Test Harness](http://graniteriverlabs.com/thread/) is maintained via the Thread Group by GRL.  OpenThread's interface to the Thread Test Harness is implemented in [tools/harness-thci/OpenThread.py](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/tools/harness-thci/OpenThread.py).  There, you can see that the PSKd is indeed a string, but that string is interpreted as a raw binary string (i.e. ascii values). ```

$ git clean -xfd
$ git checkout -- .
$ ./bootstrap
$ make -f examples/Makefile-efr32 COMMISSIONER=1 COAP=1 JOINER=1 DHCP6_CLIENT=1 DHCP6_SERVER=1 BOARD=BRD4161A
```

Yes, absolutely, go ahead with the introduction for NAPALM, I like the idea.

Being open to something new, I just took the chance to propose you what we consider a more complete technology.

The community is using Ansible because back in time (about 2 years ago perhaps) it was the best solution. Salt existed, but available only for servers, not for other type of devices. But not anymore and brings the all advantages for all types of devices now.

Easier is not always the answer in networking (e.g.: for us it would be way easier (and cheaper) to setup a vMX instead of buying MX480s). But performance is what it does matter.
Moreover, I assume you are not working right on Windows 7 (as 45% of the world) just because most of the people use it. You use what fits you the best.

And anyway, I don't see what can be easier than executing 2 lines (3 till NAPALM will become part of Saltstack) to setup the whole environment you need. The rest are just config files, specific to your network only.

And don't take me wrong: we don't support a technology just because its name begins with "S" instead of "A". We don't support "a" technology, we want a better Internet and we mean it.
Salt, we simply find it as the best you can get right now, why would we miss this chance, especially when it covers absolutely all your needs? Isn't fun that technology changes so fast?

However, here it might not be the right place for such topic. For further discussions / ideas / reports, feel free to open an issue in [napalm-salt](https://github.com/napalm-automation/napalm-salt). Cheers.

@WSQi 
The ``onecmd()`` method was never intended to be user-facing and because of that we made a change regarding the type of argument it takes.  It now takes a ``Statement`` instance from **parsing.py** instead of a ``str``.

For the sort of thing you are trying to do, we recommend that you use the ``onecmd_plus_hooks()`` method instead of ``onecmd()`` to run a single command or ``runcmds_plus_hooks()`` if you wish to run multiple commands.

``onecmd_plus_hooks()`` does the following:
1. Parses the single string argument passed to it as a cmd2 command, including arguments and possible redireciton/pipeing
2. Runs all of the application lifecycle methods like ``precmd``, ``postcmd``, etc. at the proper time
3. Handles redirection and/or piping if enabled
4. Measures command execution time if that feature is turned on
5. Runs the actual command using ``onecmd``
6. Deals with exceptions

If for some reason you really don't want all of the extra stuff provided by ``onecmd_plus_hooks``, then you can slightly modify your ``main()`` to parse the string into a ``Statement`` like so:
```Python
def main() -> None:
	c = Command()
	if len(sys.argv) > 1:
		c.onecmd(c._complete_statement(' '.join(sys.argv[1:])))
	else:
		c.cmdloop()
```

Actually, thinking a bit more about this, I find problematic that ``enum_`` is no longer a ``py::object``. This will definitely break a few of my projects that stick aliases of enumerations into other (sub-) modules to mirror ``using`` namespace statements on the C++ side.

Some food for thought:

> having a global runtime option could change the value for other libraries (if library A uses the proposed "basic" PyGILState_* API and library B uses the advanced API, one of them could clobber the other.) What I found during my testing is that this doesn't happen ...

Yeah, I was thinking ahead a bit with that concern:  It doesn't happen because, right now, the `options` class state stack is via a static variable, as you found.  I don't think, however, as a runtime option that this is the right approach—the `options` class was really meant to control how pybind handles bind definitions; its main purpose is to allow you to do something like `{ options local_opts; local_opts.enable_function_signatures(); /* ... module definitions ... */; }` to control how those definitions are applied.  (It should probably have been named `definition_options` in retrospect).  With an `options`-based approach you'd run into trouble if, for example, one module has a `options local;` to set some unrelated options, but loads your module in its definition code: the localized scope gil choice option would be reset at the end of the first module's definition scope.  I don't see that sort of scoped interface helping at all here (instead it just gets in the way).

A much more natural place for this sort of global runtime control is in `internals`.  That data *is* shared across modules (at least for modules with the same layout, which typically means the same major/minor version).  It might even be worthwhile storing similarly to how we store `internals` but *outside* `internals` itself so that it can apply even across modules compiled with different pybind versions.

---

But back to the core issue.  The biggest issue, as I see it, is that we *are* forcing the use of `pybind11::gil_scoped_release`: most notably in the `OVERLOAD` macros, though I also see another use in `functional.h`.  We should fix that.

As to what that fix looks like: I don't think that either the `PYBIND11_GIL_SCOPED_ACQUIRE` or `set_gil_scoped_acquire` approachs are particularly nice or that they entirely solve the issue: you can *still* get a deadlock and/or interpreter error fairly easily if two modules do different things w.r.t the GIL type to use.  Your module may set the define, but if another module doesn't you're right back to the same issue.

I don't personally have much experience with the GIL intricacies, so maybe there's a reason this wouldn't work, but we ought to be able to detect whether a Python "simple" GIL or a pybind "advanced" GIL is already acquired, and if so, we could have a default `pybind11::new_gil_scoped_acquire` (a new class—and just a placeholder name) which just extends whichever approach is currently in effect.  That would, I think, solve the issue for `OVERLOAD` and `functional`.  As @Erotemic points out, most modules don't really care about the implementation, they just want a GIL, and that's what this new class would do.

We could then rename `gil_scoped_acquire` to `pybind11::advanced_gil_acquire` (or something along those lines) with a typedef for backwards compatibility.  A new `pybind11::basic_gil_acquire`, as described earlier in the issue, would provide the CPython-compatibile implementation.  We might still want something like `pybind11::require_gil<basic_scoped_acquire>()` or `pybind11::require_gil<gil_scoped_acquire>()` set the default—and also to double as a runtime-check against two modules requiring incompatible gil implementations.

I thought about methods of dealing with this without needing extra macros when I was in there for PR #400, and really couldn't come up with any obviously good way to solve it.  The main issue is that we need to `return` from the macro, and propagate that to actually return from the caller.  A couple things that I considered that might work:

- Have an `overload` function that takes arguments as the usual `template... Extra` types that returns a `std::pair<bool, ret_type>`, where the "return now" (i.e. overload found) is captured in the bool.  That gets a little messier when `ret_type` is a reference type, because you need to use an `std::pair<bool, std::reference_wrapper<ret_type>>`, *and* the caller has to worry about unpacking the pair, but it might still work.
- Alternatively, and this is probably the better option, modify the base macro to take the whole overload call, rather than just the call arguments, as a macro parameter instead of the arguments; the default use (by the existing macros) would become: `PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_BASE(ret_type, cname, name, overload(__VA_ARGS__))`, but then people with other needs could invoke, for example, `PYBIND11_OVERLOAD_BASE(ret_type, cname, name, overload<pybind11::return_value_policy>(arg1, arg2, arg3))`.  (I haven't tested that, but I'm pretty sure the `,`s will be okay there since they are nested inside brackets).

Looks like what's going on is when you run `dnf install certbot-nginx`, it installs both the Python 2 and Python 3 version of Certbot but only the Python 2 version of the Nginx plugin. Fedora has configured the executable "certbot" to refer to the Python 3 version where the Nginx plugin wasn't installed.

I've reported this [here](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1497315).

In the meantime, you can either:

1. Use the executable "certbot-2".
2. Run `dnf install python3-certbot-nginx`.

I'm closing this issue in favor of tracking this bug in the Fedora package on Bugzilla, but let me know if there's anything else I can do to help.

Plus, there's really good support in Linux for integrating LDAP into the system user setup directly - so users can just use PAM (which is supported in not just JupyterHub, but ssh and a lot of other tools) to log in. You can see http://www.tldp.org/HOWTO/archived/LDAP-Implementation-HOWTO/pamnss.html and lots of other documentation on the web on how to set up LDAP to provide user accounts for your system. Those are all very widely used, much more secure & more widely documented - and I would recommend you use them rather than have JupyterHub create local accounts.

See https://github.com/jupyterhub/ldapauthenticator/issues/19 for prior discussion on this.

Thank you very much for the patch, and I appreciate it - but am not going to include this in functionality on account of it being insecure.

We need to choose /one internal/ representation and *stick to it*. The goal here is to avoid encoding errors, e.g.:

```python
>>> b'\x00' + u'0'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: can't concat str to bytes
```

> From my point of view, the only reason for that is easing operations with the binary data (e.g. concatenating/composing it with other binary data in a smartcontract call or signing), but it's done so rarely compared to standalone usage (e.g. pex/logging, ext-library calls [web3 and friends], rest endpoints), and for these rare first cases, there're plenty of functions to help on this.

There will *always* be a mismatch on the internal and external representations, that is what encoders are for, changing what we use internally will never fix this.

If we change to `str` we still have to agree on a representation, `0x` prefix or not? checksummed or not? And once that is agreed upon the libraries/IO boundaries mismatches will have to be fixed and the data must be re-encoded. This will make web3 compatible and UDP/signing incompatible, effectively it only moved the format mismatch without any benefit.

You may argue on the frequency of such things, but IMO the re-work is not even close to be worth it.

> I think we could at least re-discuss this sandwich-architecture decision.

The point of a decision like this is to make the code less error prone and avoid bike shedding like this, IMO we should not re-discuss this.

I guess this is implemented in the same way as the Keysight 33xxx series driver in `KeysightAgilent_33XXX.py` where it uses 9.9e37 as the response to signal infinity? 

It could probably be solved in the same way using a val mapping that looks something like this

```
        def val_parser(parser: type, inputstring: str) -> str:
            """
            Parses return values from instrument. Meant to be used when a query
            can return a meaningful finite number or a numeric representation
            of infinity

            Args:
                parser: Either int or float, what to return in finite
                    cases
                inputstring: The raw return value
            """

            inputstring = inputstring.strip()

            if float(inputstring) == 9.9e37:
                output = float('inf')
            else:
                output = float(inputstring)
                if parser == int:
                    output = parser(output)

            return output
```

Fixed by https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/commit/3452198320bf030cb75e4582ada79fa2308408e7

Those require already knowing what the URL is before you can reverse it, whereas I want to do it the other way: I don't know what the URL is, but I know what generates it. Sort of.

I'm basically looking for how to call something similar to `login_url = social_auth.url("social:begin", "google-oauth2")`, so using the same code that is used by the templating system in order to stay pythonic, without having to do that *through* the templating system. 

To explain why this code exists: this is a server that is both a normal Django instance combining site content and DRF data APIs, and so also acts as a login broken for various third party clients. They can call `/api/v1/login?original_url=...` which should cause a redirect chain: 

- login redirects to google login
- google login redirects to the `completed` callback url
- the post-login handler (pipeline function) then redirects back to the client's original url

nowhere in this chain is a page ever rendered by Django, it's all pure redirect logic. As such, the code that services this end point needs something in place to get the login URL through plain python code. I'm "abusing" the template system to make sure that happens right now, but a direct call to the same function that the templates use would be infinitely cleaner.

```
import re

from streamlink.plugin import Plugin
from streamlink.plugin.api import http


_url_re = re.compile(r'''https?://(?:www\.)?teamliquid\.net/video/streams/''')
_embed_re = re.compile(r'''<iframe class="videoplayer" src="([^\"]+)"''')
_afreecaRe = re.compile('View on Afreeca')
_twitchRe = re.compile('View on Twitch.tv')


class Teamliquid(Plugin):
    @classmethod
    def can_handle_url(cls, url):
        return _url_re.match(url)

    def _get_streams(self):
        res = http.get(self.url)
        #print(res.text)
        ## Res.text gives us the html stuff
        afreeca= _afreecaRe.findall(res.text)
        twitch= _twitchRe.findall(res.text)
        #match = _embed_re.search(res.text)
        if afreeca:
            streamAddressRe=re.compile('http://play.afreeca.com/[^">/]+')
            url=streamAddressRe.findall(res.text)[0]
            return self.session.streams(url)
        
        elif twitch:
            streamAddressRe=re.compile('http://www.twitch.tv/[^">/]+')
            url=streamAddressRe.findall(res.text)[0]
            return self.session.streams(url)
            
__plugin__ = Teamliquid

```

__Currently it does not work with Windows XP or Windows Vista, as it uses `ffmpeg 3.2`__

I tested every stable release of `ffmpeg` from 3.0 to 3.2.1 (on Windows XP SP3, Vista SP2, and 10), HFR video and 1080p, 1440p worked fine. However, there appears to be an issue playing 4K content in versions less than 3.2. I am in the process of build a version of `ffmpeg 3.2` that should work on Windows XP, and Vista. I will update the installer once I have done that.

It's easier to directly link to the latest version in the docs, @thinkpad4 makes a good point - it's better for our users. I have managed to do that by creating a "latest" version which gets updated (https://dl.bintray.com/streamlink/streamlink-nightly/streamlink-latest.exe). Bintray seems to sort the list in ascending order too, as it's just a list of files in this case - latest at the bottom. 

I am going to rename the files to include the date (`streamlink-0.5.0-20170505.exe`), and configure the builds to be updated only **once** per day (in true nightly fashion). The Bintray version numbers will be the date (`2017.05.05`), with the description containing the short commit hash. Only updating once per day will help us keep under the storage limit for a while, but I'd also like to push forward with removing the `ffmpeg.exe` from the installer and get it downloading on-demand during the installation.

Yet another way to get this working, but without installing a different node version (and without waiting for a JupyterLab build with jupyterlab/jupyterlab#4632). In my setup, I have everything in a virtualenv.

1. Upgrade your system yarn to 1.7.0 or higher: `npm install -g yarn` It will probably do something like this:
```
bash-3.2$ npm install -g yarn
/usr/local/bin/yarn -> /usr/local/lib/node_modules/yarn/bin/yarn.js
/usr/local/bin/yarnpkg -> /usr/local/lib/node_modules/yarn/bin/yarn.js
```
1. Hang onto the complete path to `yarn.js` that `npm` reported. (It might be a different path on your system to what I've pasted above.)
1. Find where the `jupyterlab` Python module is installed; in my virtualenv, it's in `lib/python3.6/site-packages/jupyterlab/`
1. Inside the module, edit `staging/.yarnrc`, which will look like:
```
yarn-path "./yarn.js"
```
Change the value to the complete path to `yarn.js` that you grabbed in step 2.
1. `jupyter labextension install` should now work!

A lot to unpack here.

Another data point: I *really* dislike the idea that a nursery's `__aexit__` silently waits for the child tasks. Explicit is better than implicit, and people don't expect the end of a `__with__` block to hang around indefinitely. (At least I don't.)

Given that, I'd rather have `__aexit__` implicitly call `nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()`. If you want to wait for the remaining tasks, well, as soon as we have introspection the caller can iterate over the running tasks and wait for them the way it wants to. For instance, when I end a TCP server I'd like to cancel the remaining tasks after a couple of seconds, not immediately.

NB: instead of explicitly hooking up a supervisor task which replaces _some_ default behavior, I'd rather subclass the nursery (cf. #340 – we don't need an explicit `open_nursery()` function, just use `Nursery()`). That way you get the default behavior when you want it – simply call `super()`.

fixes it for the HEAD but would break 541,  for delete requests the 199 content length comes straight from the moto server, so if the content-length 199 is  incorrect for a DELETE request, then the bug is upstream in moto. 
Could add a conditional reset of the content-length for delete requests of course

Which client are you using to invoke the API Gateway endpoint? The server-side output seems to indicate that it was actually the client that closed the connection (?). Are you also receiving an error on the client side?

I was able to to reproduce the issue with a long-running Lambda that is triggered from an S3 notification, however, that case is different because the S3->Lambda integration uses an actual Lambda `invoke` service invocation, whereas the API Gateway->Lambda integration in fact performs an in-memory call and not a invocation over the network (which is actually an anti-pattern, but that's a different issue :) ).

Is it possible that your client uses a socket read timeout and then fails (closes the connection) after not receiving any data for 60 seconds?

Not using WGS84 is a direct violation of the [GeoJSON RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7946).

> The coordinate reference system for all GeoJSON coordinates is a
   geographic coordinate reference system, using the World Geodetic
   System 1984 (WGS 84) [WGS84] datum, with longitude and latitude units
   of decimal degrees.

I've experienced ArcGIS violating this. I think the best place to document this would be in ["common issues"](https://github.com/sentinelsat/sentinelsat/blob/master/docs/common_issues.rst), potentially with a list of software that produces non-standard GeoJSON.

There are multiple ways to handle a per event validation.
- Create a variable (`VariableAggregate`) with the required clean up on the event field and then use that variable.
- Use `When` in `Field` for validation conditions.
- Define a `valid_event` field (as you mentioned) and use that in `When`. This can be go in `VariableAggregate` if you don't want to save it.
- If its ok to ignore fields that have errors (e.g. missing) you can choose to do nothing and blurr already handles this. In case of missing event fields in source the `Field` evaluation that uses it will be skipped and an error is logged in debug logs.

1) One thing to note is that this design describes things closer to the backend - i.e. how execution needs to be handled. Another representation of RNN would be to describe what happens to a single sequence and rely on auto-batching logic to handle sequences padding, masking, etc (what @salexspb is talking about)

The above "single sequence" representation is also interesting to explore and maybe we should add it to the spec. But imho, we should add it only in addition to plain-while-loop representation proposed here. It might be interesting to abstract in ONNX the path of converting between the two representations at some point.

2) For optimizations of bidirectional RNNs it might make sense to add "reverse" to LoopIndexTensor that works only if fixed trip count is provided. It'd avoid manually flipping the tensor or doing the math on loop_var

3) To @gdiamos concern, one of the value of ONNX in my opinion is being able to represent DL compute patterns as close to reality as possible to enable specialized optimizations in backends. For example, some backends might have a support for RNN as a fixed-size for loop. If we represent RNN as a generic while loop with iteration conditions expressed as ops it makes it harder to pattern-match RNN-like fixed for loop. If we go with more restrictive implementation, it's always easier to lower it to a more elemental one.

It's similar philosophy to Gemm op. Yes, we can represent it as matrix multiplies + transposes + addition. But frequent usage of particular pattern in practice means that it's justifiable to keep more higher-level implementation to make optimizations easier.

> As to whether we call these things namespaces, libraries, modules, or packages, I'm not married to any one term. To avoid picking a name, I will use LNMP throughout my comments as a placeholder for whichever one we land on.

I think in the spec text, I used the term "vendor (operator) extension", to make it clear that the third parties that are most likely to define extra operators are the backend vendors, who would actually be in the business of implementing them. The reason I don't like library/module/package is because it brings the intuition that *users* could write libraries of operators, composed out of more primitive ones, that could portably be reused over many backends (and then you'd have some sort of package manager, etc. etc. etc.) This might be a legitimate problem to solve, but it's not the one we're trying to solve with operator versioning.

> I prefer declaration, but am fine with specification or contract. Definition implies a specific implementation, which I'm not a fan of.

I'm absolutely fine with declaration/specification. They are *solely* called definitions right now, because that is the internal naming convention in ONNX library. I can rename `def` to `spec` in the library to make this clear.

I am very surprised that the methods has such a small effect.

Without inspecting the dataset is difficult to answer but BRCA1 rings a bell... there seem to be some cell cycle signal that has high velocity in the control. If these are cells in culture, the cell cycle signal might be quite strong and also since samples are separated by days other processes might take over velocity. The only way to diagnose problems might be to inspect several phase portrait... we will soon include some extra diagnostic tools to allow explaining the sources of the projected arrows but it is not ready yet.

A brain storming of suggestions / think that I would do if I were to analyse this dataset:
 - Try to perform a  feature selection that considers only genes with some significant dynamics over the time points but exclude the genes that seem to be expressed only at one time point (these kinf of genes can come from batch effects or they might be a symptom that the gene's dynamics is too fast to catch with respect of the sampling frequency).

- Project the velocity on the same PCA on which knn has been calculated (see vlm.pcs) ? What are the genes with major loadings (see vlm.pca)? Does it make sense to visualize the data in that subspace?
 
- Try eliminate the signal from the cell cycle and use `filter_genes_good_fit` to remove genes that have uncorrelated phase portraits. 

- On how many genes are you calculating velocity? Is there a strong size variation between condition?

- Reduce b_sight=k*8, b_maxl=k*4,  to   b_sight=k*5, b_maxl=k*3, 

- Are the outliers cells with super strong velocity also the one with smaller total unspliced? Because maybe size normalisation is inflating few genes and generating this artefact normalize_total had the argument  min_perc_U, increase it to exclude those cells from normalisation (leavign them with the same number of molecules)

And just a suggestion for visualization, the plot is very packed when plotting try int(vlm.embedding.shape[0]/2) instead of /1 to show half of the arrows.

To answer your second question, the simpler way I can think of: 
- take the source code for the function `calculate_grid_arrows ` and `plot_grid_arrows` and join them
- you enclose most of the code in a loop over the populations (for i in vlm.cluster_uid) 
- than inside the loop assign `embedding` to getattr(self, embed)[vlm.cluster_ix ==i, :], do the same for delta_embedding. It might be useful to have a new variable `embedding_all` that you use when embedding is used only to calculate extremities of the grid
- at every iteration plot on a different subplot.
If you think this plot it is a nice feature and an useful feature to add, you should open another post for a feature request, explain in detail what the function should do and the general API, and we will try to write it and integrate it into velocyto.

> @rbucker881 I wouldn't take offense to the initial 'read the Guide' comment - that's a trope of zeromq support, particularly on the IRC channel.  It's ~always the first response to questions, and meant to be quite friendly.
> 
> As for your test case, the second endpoint is most certainly valid, as described in [this section](http://zguide.zeromq.org/page:all#Plugging-Sockets-Into-the-Topology) of the Guide, specifically:
> 
> * [ØMQ connections] exist when a client does zmq_connect(3) to an endpoint, whether or not a server has already done zmq_bind(3) to that endpoint.
> 
> The messages are being queued in memory, waiting for the second server to bind.  This is why the client does not exit at the end of your script - it sits running, waiting for the second server to bind and receive its messages.  If you start a server on the second endpoint at any later time, it will receive its messages and the client will exit cleanly.
> 
> Another example: if you set HWM=1 on the client, then that's the maximum size of the in-memory queue, so your missing endpoint will only be assigned its first message, and your running one will get messages 1,3-9.  The default HWM is infinite, so messages keep being assigned to the second endpoint, trusting that it will come up eventually.
